question: If you are using eye glasses of power $2\,D$, your near point is
explanation:
Given, $P=2 D$
Focal length of lens, $f = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{{100}}{2} = 50\,cm$
By lens formula
$\dfrac{1}{f}=\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{u}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{50}=\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{(-25)} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{50}=\dfrac{1}{v}+\dfrac{1}{25}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{v}=\dfrac{1}{50}-\dfrac{1}{25}=\dfrac{-1}{50}$
$ \Rightarrow \nu = - 50\,cm$
subject: Physics

question:

The eyepiece of a telescope is used to view the real image formed by the objective lens at its focus.

explanation: Statement A is true as the eyepiece of a telescope magnifies the real image formed at the focal plane of the objective lens. Statement B is also true as a typical refracting telescope uses convex lenses for both the objective and the eyepiece. Correct option is (1)

question: A telescope has an objective of focal length 100 $cm$ and an eye-piece of focal length 5 $cm$ . The magnifying power of the telescope for normal setting, is
explanation:
Magnification of a telescope for normal setting is
$m=\dfrac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}=\dfrac{100}{5}=20$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: What minimum separation between two objects a human eye would be able to resolve, if the eye pupil diameter is 2$mm$ and the two objects are  $20{\text{ }}m$  away from the eye? (Assume, human eye to be equivalent to a convex lens and consider the average wavelength of light as  $600{\text{ }}nm$)
explanation:
Given, diameter of pupil,
$d=2 {~mm}=2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}$
$D=20 {~m}, \lambda=600 {~nm}=6 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
For any aperture, limit of resolution is given as,
$\dfrac{y}{D} \geq \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{d} \Rightarrow y \geq \dfrac{1.22 \lambda D}{d}$
Putting the values
$y \geq \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-7} \times 20 \times 1.22}{2 \times 10^{-3}} m$
$\Rightarrow y \geq 7.32 {~mm}$
Hence, minimum required separation is $7.32{\text{ }}mm.$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: Let $S_{1}$ be the amount of Rayleigh scattered light of wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ that of light of wavelength $\lambda_{2}$ from a particle of size $a$. Which of the following statement is true?
explanation:
Condition for scattering is
$\lambda \gg a$
and according to Rayleigh scattering, intensity (or amount) of scattered wavelength is inversely proportional to fourth power of the wavelength of incident wave, i. e.
$S \propto \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{4}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{4}$ and $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2} \gg a$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A microscope has an objective of focal length $2\,cm$, eyepiece of focal length $4\,cm$ and the tube length of $40\,cm$. If the distance of distinct vision of eye is $25\,cm$, the magnification in the microscope is
explanation: $m=\dfrac{L}{f_{o}} \times \dfrac{D}{f_{e}}=\dfrac{40}{2} \times \dfrac{25}{4}=125$

question:
In determining the refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope, the following reading are tabulated.
(a) Reading of travelling microscope for ink mark $ = 5.123\,cm$
(b) Reading of travelling microscope for ink mark through glass slab $ = 6.123\,cm$
(c) Reading of travelling microscope for chalk dust on glass slab $ = 8.123\,cm$. From the data, the refractive index of a glass slab is
explanation:
For near normal vision
$n=\dfrac{\text { Real thickness }}{\text { Apparent thickness }}$
$=\dfrac{R_{3}-R_{1}}{R_{3}-R_{2}}$$=\dfrac{8.123-5.123}{8.123-6.123}=\dfrac{3}{2}=1.500$
So correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

explanation: Real, enlarged and inverted.

question: An astronomical telescope has objective and eyepiece of focal length $50 {~m}$ and $10 {~m}$ respectively. To view an object $250 {~m}$ away from the objective, the lenses must be separated by a distance
explanation:
Given: ${f_o} = 50\,m,{\rm{ }}{f_e} = 10\,m$
For objective,
$\dfrac{1}{v_{o}}-\dfrac{1}{u_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{f_{o}}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{v_{0}}-\dfrac{1}{-250}=\dfrac{1}{50}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{v_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{50}-\dfrac{1}{250}=\dfrac{5-1}{250}=\dfrac{1}{62.5}$
$v_{0}=62.5 {~m}$
For normal adjustment, the image formed by objective will be at the focus of the eyepiece.
$\therefore \quad {L}={v}_{{o}}+{f}_{{e}}=72.5 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question:
A man with normal near point ${(25 {~cm})}$ reads a book using a magnifying glass of focal length
5 $cm$ . The closest distance at which he can read the book when viewing through the magnifying glass is
explanation:
Here, focal length ${(f)=5 {~m}}$
${v=D=-25 {~cm}}$
To find ${u}$, we have
${\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{u}=\dfrac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{-25}-\dfrac{1}{u}=\dfrac{1}{5} \Rightarrow-\dfrac{1}{u}=\dfrac{6}{25}}$
${\Rightarrow u=-\dfrac{25}{6}=-4.2 {~cm}}$
${\because}$ The closest distance considered to be object distance.
${\therefore}$ The closest distance will be $ - 4.2cm$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

explanation: A very small part of light energy is lost from an optical fibre due to absorption or due to light leaving the fibre area resulting scattering of light sideways by impurities in the glass fibre.

question: Magnification of a compound microscope is 30. Focal length of eyepiece is $5\,cm$ and the image is formed at a distance of distinct vision $25\,cm$. The magnification of the objective lens is
explanation:
The image formed in a
microscope at least distance of
distinct vision $(D = 25\,cm)$ has
magnification, $M = {M_o} \times {M_e}$
where ${M_o}$ is magnification of
objective lens and ${M_e}$ that of
eyepiece.
Given, $\,\,\,{f_e} = 5\,cm,D = 25\,cm$
and $\,\,\,M = 30$
Also, $\,M = {M_o}\left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,30 = {M_o} \times \left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{5}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {M_o} = \frac{{30}}{6} = 5$
subject: Physics

question: A person who can see things most clearly at a distance of $10\,cm,$ requires spectacles to enable to see clearly things at a distance of $30\,cm$. What should be the focal length of the spectacles?
explanation:
Since, the person is not able to see distant objects beyond $10\,cm$ so, he should use concave lens in his spectacles. Concave lens will form the image of object distant $30\,cm$, at $10\,cm$ it means $u = - 30\,cm$, $v = - 10\,cm$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{ - 10}} - \frac{1}{{ - 30}} = - \frac{2}{{30}}$
or $\frac{1}{f} = - \frac{1}{{15}}$
So, $f = - 15\,cm$
Minus sign signifies that lens used in concave.
subject: Physics

explanation:

question: For a telescope, larger the diameter of the objective lens
explanation:
The resolving power of a telescope
is its ability to show two distant
closely lying objects as just separate.
The reciprocal of resolving power is
the limit of resolution of the telescope.
Limit of resolution $ = 1.22\frac{\lambda }{d}rad,$
where $\lambda$ is wavelength and $d$ the
aperture (diameter). Hence, to reduce
the limit of resolution of a telescope, we
must use objective lens of large aperture $(d).$
Larger the aperture of the objective
lens, smaller the limit of resolution or
greater the resolving power of the telescope.
subject: Physics

question: A leaf which contains only green pigments, is illuminated by a laser light of wavelength $0.6328 \mu \mathrm{m}$. It would appear to be
explanation:
If an object reflects the colour of light incident on it, it will appear with that colour but if object absorbs the colour of light, it will appear to be black.
Since, the given wavelength does not belong to green, so it will be absorbed by the leaf and hence, it would appear to be black.
subject: Physics

question: The focal length of objective and eye lens of astronomical telescope are respectively $2\,m$ and $5\,cm$. Final image is formed at (1) least distance of distinct vision (2) infinity. The magnifying power in both cases will be:
explanation:
When the final image is at the least distance of distinct vision, then
${m=-\dfrac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}\left(1+\dfrac{f_{e}}{D}\right)}$
${=\dfrac{200}{5}\left(1+\dfrac{5}{25}\right)}$
${=\dfrac{200 \times 6}{5 \times 5}=-48}$
When the final image is at infinity, then
$m=-\dfrac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}=\dfrac{200}{5}=-40$
subject: Physics

question: If the focal length of objective and eye lens are $1.2\,cm$ and $3\,cm$ respectively and the object is put $1.25\,cm$ away from the objective lens and the final image is formed at infinity. The magnifying power of the microscope is
explanation:
When the final image is formed at infinity, we have
$m_{\infty}=\dfrac{v_{0}}{u_{0}} \times \dfrac{D}{f_{e}}$
From ${\dfrac{1}{f_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}-\dfrac{1}{u_{0}}}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{(+1.2)}=\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}-\dfrac{1}{(-1.25)} \Rightarrow v_{0}=30 {~cm}}$
${\therefore\left|m_{\infty}\right|=\dfrac{30}{1.25} \times \dfrac{25}{3}=200}$
subject: Physics

question: An astronomical telescope has an eyepiece of focal-length $5\,cm$. If the angular magnification in normal adjustment is 10 when final image is at least distance of distinct vision ${(25 {~cm})}$ from eye piece, then angular magnification will be
explanation:
For normal adjustment
$m=-\dfrac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}$
When final image is at least distance of distinct vision from eyepiece,
$m^{\prime}=-\dfrac{f_{0}}{f_{e}}\left(1+\dfrac{f_{e}}{d}\right)=10\left(1+\dfrac{5}{25}\right)=12$
subject: Physics

question: In a compound microscope, the focal length of two lenses are $1.5\,cm$ and $6.25\,cm$. If an object is placed at $2\,cm$ from objective and the final image is formed at $25\,cm$ from eye lens the distance between two lenses is -
explanation:
${{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,{\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{objective}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{eyepiece}}}$
${\frac{1}{{{f_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{u_0}}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_e}}}}$
${ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{1.5}} = \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{ - 2}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{6.25}} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{{{v_e}}}}$
${ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} = \frac{1}{{1.5}} - \frac{1}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{{6.25}}}$
${ \Rightarrow {v_0} = 6\,cm\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow {u_e} = - 5\,cm}$
So, ${L=\left|v_{0}\right|+\left|u_{e}\right|=(6+5) {cm}=11 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics

question: When an astronomical telescope is focussed on a distant star, the distance of the eye piece from the objective is $60\,cm$. When focussed on a distant flag pole, the eye piece must be drawn out $10\,cm$. If the focal length of the eye piece is $5\,cm$, what will be distance of the pole from the objective? Assume that the eye is focussed for infinity
explanation:
We have ${f_0} + {f_e} = 60\,cm\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given ${f_{e}=5 {~cm}}$
${\therefore \quad f_{0}=55 {~cm} \quad\left[\because f_{0}+f_{e}=60 {~cm}\right]}$
In the second case,
${v_{0}=(60+10)-f_{e}=65 {~cm}}$
From the relation,
${\dfrac{1}{v_{o}}-\dfrac{1}{u_{o}}=\dfrac{1}{f_{o}}}$
we have, ${\dfrac{1}{65}-\dfrac{1}{u_{o}}=\dfrac{1}{55}}$
${\therefore u_{o}=-357.5 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics

question: An astronomical telescope has objective and eyepiece of focal lengths $40\,cm$ and $4\,cm$ respectively. To view an object $200\,cm$ away from the objective, the lenses must be separated by a distance
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955636/preptracker/questions/q_33104_explanation_1764955635694.png Applying lens formula for objective lens
${\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}-\dfrac{1}{u_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{f_{0}}}$
${\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}-\dfrac{1}{-200}=\dfrac{1}{40}}$
${\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{40}-\dfrac{1}{200}}$
${\dfrac{1}{v_{0}}=\dfrac{5-1}{200}=\dfrac{4}{200}=\dfrac{1}{50}}$
${v_{0}=50 {~cm}}$
Image must from at the focus of eyepiece lens so, for normal adjustment, the distance between objective and eyepiece (length of tube) will be,
${l=v_{o}+f_{e}=50+4=54 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics

question: A person has been using spectacles of power -1.0 dioptre for distant vision and a separate reading glass of power 2.0 dioptres. What is the least distance of distinct vision for this person?
explanation:
For long sightedness or distant vision,
$f = \frac{{1m}}{P} = \frac{{100}}{{ - 1}} = - 100\,cm$
Therefore, image is formed at $100\,cm$ which is far point of the person. Now, for nearby objects person uses glasses of power $+2 D$
or $f = \frac{{100\,cm}}{2} = 50\,cm$
The normal distance of distinct vision is $25\,cm$ which is different for the presbyopia person.
Here, $f = 50\,cm$ and $u = - 25\,cm$.
Using lens formula,
$\dfrac{1}{f}=\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{u}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{50\,cm}} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{{25\,cm}}$ or $\dfrac{1}{v}=\dfrac{1}{50}-\dfrac{1}{25}$
or $v = - 50\,cm$
$=\dfrac{1-2}{50}=\dfrac{-1}{50}$
$\therefore$ Least distance for distinct vision $ = 50\,cm$.
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens of focal length $40\,\,cm$ forms an image of an extended source of light on a photoelectric cell. A current $I$ is produced. The lens is replaced by another convex lens having the same diameter but focal length $20\,\,cm$. The photoelectric current now is :
explanation: Photoelectric current $\propto$ Photoelectrons. Photoelectrons $\propto$ Intensity of the incident light.As amonut of energy incident on cell is same, so current will remain same.
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Microscope magnifies the image.

Reason: Angular magnification for image is more than object in microscope.
explanation: Microscopes are optical instruments designed to form magnified images of small, nearby objects. This magnification increases the visual angle subtended by the image when viewed, making the object appear larger and more distinct. Hence, the angular magnification for the image is greater than that for the original object. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Owls can move freely during night.

Reason: They have large number of rods on their retina.
explanation: In human eyes, there are two types of receptors: rods and cones. Rods are highly sensitive to low light conditions, making them suitable for night vision. Owls have a significant number of rods in their retina, allowing them to move freely in the dark. So correct option is $(1)$.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A reflecting type telescope is preferred in astronomy.

Reason: Weight of the mirrors are less as compared to the lenses.
explanation: Manufacturing a large-aperture lens free from aberrations is difficult, making largeaperture lenses impractical for telescopes. Reflecting telescopes, using parabolic mirrors to mitigate aberrations, are preferred in astronomy due to light-gathering power. So correct option is (3).

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A normal human eye can clearly see all the objects at different distances.

Reason: The human eye has the ability to suitably adjust the focal length of its lens to a certain extent.
explanation: The human eye can adjust its focal length through ciliary muscles for focusing on objects at varying distances. However, there's a minimum distance, the least distance for clear vision. So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: For the sensitivity of a camera, its aperture should be reduced.

Reason: Smaller the aperture, image focussing is also sharp.
explanation: Large apertures can result in blurred images due to aberrations. However, by reducing the aperture, clear images can be obtained, thereby increasing the camera's sensitivity. So correct option is (3).

question: A compound microscope a magnification of 24 . The focal length of the eyepiece is $5\,cm$. The final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision. The magnification produced by the objective is
explanation:
For a compound microscope,
$M=m_{0} \times m_{e}$
Also, the final image is formed at $D$.
So, $M=m_{0}\left(1+\dfrac{D}{f_{e}}\right)$
$m_{0}=\dfrac{M}{1+\dfrac{D}{f_{e}}}=\dfrac{24}{1+\dfrac{25}{5}}=\dfrac{24}{6}
\Rightarrow {m_0} = 4$
subject: Physics

question: A certain far-sighted person cannot see object closer to the eye than $100\,cm$. The power of the lens which will enable him to read at a distance of  $25\,cm$ will be
explanation:
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}$$ \Rightarrow \,f = - 20cm$
$u\, = \, - 25\,cm{\mkern 1mu} ,\,v = \, - 100\,cm \Rightarrow \,f = - 20\,cm$
and $P = \frac{{100}}{{20}} = 5D$

question: A myopic person cannot see objects lying beyond $2\,m$. The focal length and power of the lens required to remove this defect will be
explanation:
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$
If the object is at infinity then the image should be formed at $2\,m$ from the lens.
$\frac{1}{{ - 2}} - \frac{1}{\infty } = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f = - 2\,m\,\& \,P = - 0.5D$

question: The power of a lens used to remove the myopic defect of eye is 0.66D. The far point of this eye is  (nearly)
explanation:
Let x be the far point
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{{ - x}} - \frac{1}{\infty } = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow x = - f = - 151\,cm$

question: The length of the tube of a compound microscope is $15\,cm$. The focal length of objective and eye lenses are $1\,cm$ and  $5\,cm$ respectively. The magnifying power of microscope for relaxed vision is
explanation:
Given that
$L = 15{\mkern 1mu} cm,\,{f_0} = 1{\mkern 1mu} cm\,\& \,{f_e} = 5{\mkern 1mu} cm$
As the final image is formed at infinity
${v_0} = L - {f_e} = 10\,cm$
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{10}} - \frac{1}{1}$
$ \Rightarrow {u_0} = \frac{{ - 10}}{9}cm$
$ \Rightarrow m = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}}\left[ {\frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right] = - 45$
subject: Physics

question: A magnifying glass has $f = 12\,cm$. To produce maximum angular magnification the object should be placed at a distance of  (Least distance of clear vision  $ = 25\,cm$)
explanation:
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{12}}$
$ \Rightarrow u = 8.1\,cm$

subject: Physics

question: A hypermetropic person having near point at a distance of  $0.75\,m$ puts on spectacles of power $2.5\,D$. The near point now is at
explanation:
As, $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{{\rm{1}}}{v} - \frac{1}{u}$
Let $x$ is the new near point when the object is placed at $x$ then its image is formed at $0.75\,m$.
$ \Rightarrow 2.5 = \frac{1}{{ - 0.75}} + \frac{1}{x}$
or$x = \frac{6}{{23}}\,m = 0.26{\mkern 1mu} \,m$
subject: Physics

question: In compound microscope, magnifying power is 95 and the distance of object from objective lens is $\frac{1}{{3.8}}cm.$ The focal length of objective lens is What is $\frac{1}{4}cm.$ What is the magnification of eyepiece?
explanation:
For the objective
$\frac{1}{{{v_o}}} - \frac{1}{{ - 1/3.8}} = \frac{1}{{1/4}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{v_o}}} + 3.8 = 4$$ \Rightarrow {v_o} = 5\,cm$
Now, ${m_o} = \frac{5}{{ - \frac{1}{{3.8}}}} = - 19$
Again, $m = {m_o} \times {m_e}$
$ - 95 = - 19 \times {m_e}$$ \Rightarrow {m_e} = \frac{{95}}{{19}} = 5$
subject: Physics

question: A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length $140\,cm$ and eye piece of focal length $5.0\,cm$. The telescope is used to view a $100\,m$ tall tower  $3\,km$ away. The height of the image of the tower formed by objective lens is
explanation:
The height of the image of the tower is given by
$m = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{{{h_2}}}{{{h_1}}} \Rightarrow {h_2} = \frac{{{f_0}{h_1}}}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{{14}}{3}cm$
subject: Physics

question: The magnification produced by an astronomical telescope for normal adjustment is 10 and the length of the telescope is $1.1\,m$. The 	magnification when the image is formed at half distance of the distinct vision ($D = 25\,cm$) is
explanation:
$m = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}};\,\,\,L = {f_0} + {f_e}$$ \Rightarrow {f_e} = \frac{L}{{m + 1}}$
$ \Rightarrow {f_e} = 10\,cm$
Magnifying power of a telescope is
$m = \frac{{ - {f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{{{f_e}}}{{\left| {{v_e}} \right|}}} \right]$
Where $\left| {{v_e}} \right|$ is the position of the final image
$ = 10\left[ {1 + \frac{{10}}{{12.5}}} \right]$$ = \frac{{225}}{{12.5}} = 18$
subject: Physics

question: An astronomical telescope has magnification 15 for normal adjustment. The focal length of the eyepiece is $10\,cm$. Find the magnification if the final image is formed at $50\,cm$ from the eyepiece.
explanation:
The magnification
$m = \frac{{ - {f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{D}{{\left| {{v_e}} \right|}}} \right]$
Given that $\frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = 15,\,\left| {{v_e}} \right| = 50\,cm,\,D = 25\,cm$
$m = - 15\left[ {1 + \frac{{25}}{{50}}} \right] = - 22.5$
subject: Physics

question: A simple telescope, consisting of an objective of focal length  $60\,cm$ and a single eye lens of focal length $5\,cm$ is focused on a distant object in such a way that parallel rays comes out from the eye lens. If the object subtends an angle 2° at the objective, the angular width of the image
explanation:
Since $m = \frac{{{f_o}}}{{{f_e}}}$
Also $m = \frac{{{\rm{Angle}}\,{\rm{subtend}}\,{\rm{by}}\,{\rm{the}}\,{\rm{image}}}}{{{\rm{Angle}}\,{\rm{subtend}}\,{\rm{by}}\,{\rm{the}}\,{\rm{object}}}}$
Let $\alpha $ - Angle subtend by the object on the unaided eye
$\beta $- Angle subtend by the final image on the eye
$\therefore $ $\frac{{{f_o}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{\beta }{\alpha } \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{{{f_o} \times \alpha }}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{60 \times 2}}{5} = {24^0}$
subject: Physics

question:
It is possible that mass density of an optically denser medium may be less than that of an optically rarer medium.
Optical density depends on ratio of the speed of light in two media.
explanation: It is possible that mass density of an optically denser medium may be less than that of an optically rarer medium (optical density is the ratio of the speed of light in two media). e.g., Turpentine and water, mass density of turpentine is less than that of water but its optical density is higher.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Assertion: In a movie, ordinarily 24 frames are projected per second from one end to the other of the complete film.

Reason: The image formed on retina of eye is sustained up to 1/10 s after the removal of change of object.
explanation: When the movie is screened, the frames are displayed one after the other at the rate of 24 frames per second. The image of an object is formed on the retina. From where it is sent to the brain. If the object moves, the brain continues to show the old image for about one sixteenth of a second.

Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of  the Assertion.

Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question:
Assertion: By increasing the diameter of the objective of telescope, we can increase image brightness.

Reason: A telescope is used to provide angular magnification of distant objects.
explanation: The light gathering power (or brightness) of a telescope $ \propto {{\rm{(}}diameter{\rm{)}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{.}}$ So by increasing the objective diameter even stars may produce images of optimum  brightness. So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: If objective and eye lenses of a microscope are interchanged then it can work as telescope.

Reason: The objective of telescope has small focal length.
explanation: We cannot interchange the objective and eye lens of a microscope to make a telescope. The reason is that the focal length of lenses in microscope are very small, of the order of $mm,$  while the telescope objective has a very large focal length as compared to eye lens of microscope. So option (4) is correct.

question:
Assertion: The objective and the eye-piece of a compound microscope should have short focal lengths.

Reason: Magnifying power of a compound microscope is inversely proportional to the focal lengths of both the lenses.
explanation:
Magnification of a compound microscope is
For normal adjustment
$m = - \frac{l}{{{f_0}}}\left[ {\frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$
For least distance of clear vision
$m = - \frac{l}{{{f_0}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$
If ${f_0}\,{\rm{and}}\,{f_e}$ are small then $m$ will be large. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of  the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question:
Assertion: When viewing through a compound microscope, our eyes should be positioned not on the eyepiece but a short distance away from it for best viewing.

Reason: The image of the objective in the eyepiece is known as ‘eye-ring’ and if we position our eyes on the eye-ring and the area of the pupil of our eye is greater or equal to the area of the eye-ring, our eyes will collect all the light refracted by the objective.
explanation: Option (1) is correct.

explanation: Option (1) is correct.

question: An astronomical telescope has objective and eyepiece of focal lengths 40 $cm$ and 4 $cm$ respectively. To view an object 200 $cm$ away from the objective, the lenses must be separated by a distance:-
explanation:
Using lens formula for objective lens
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} = \frac{1}{{40}} + \frac{1}{{ - 200}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_0} = 50\,cm$
Tube length $\ell = \left| {{v_0}} \right| + {f_e} = 50 + 4 = 54\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The magnifying power of a telescope is 9. When it is adjusted for parallel rays the distance between the objective and eyepiece is $20\,cm$. The focal length of lenses are
explanation:
Given,
$Magnification = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = 9\,{\rm{and}}\,{f_0} + {f_e} = 20$
${f_0} = 9{f_e}$$ \Rightarrow 9{f_e} + {f_e} = 20$
${f_e} = 2\,cm$$ \Rightarrow \quad {f_0} = 9 \times 2 = 18\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A microscope is having objective of focal length $1\,cm$ and eyepiece of focal length $6\,cm$. If tube length is $30\,cm$ and image is formed at the least distinct vision, what is the magnification produced by the microscope? Take $D = 25\,cm$
explanation:
Focal length of objective${f_o} = 1\,cm$
Focal length of eye piece ${f_e} = 6\,cm$
Least distance of distinct vision, $D{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}25\,cm$
Tube length, $L{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}30\,cm$
When the image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision, the magnification is
$m = \frac{L}{{{f_o}}}\left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right) = \frac{{30}}{1}\left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{6}} \right) = 155$
subject: Physics

question: Calculate the focal length of a reading glass of a person  if  his distance of distinct  vision is $75\,cm$.
explanation:
The distance of normal vision is $25\,cm$. If a book to be read is at $u = - 25\,cm$, its image should be formed at $v = - 75\,cm$. Therefore, the required focal length ($f$) is given by,
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{ - 75\,cm}} - \frac{1}{{ - 25\,cm}} = \frac{2}{{75\,cm}}$
$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{{75\,cm}}{2} = 37.5{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{cm}}$
subject: Physics

question: If the focal length of objective lens is increased, then magnifying power of
explanation:
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is
$m = \frac{{ - l}}{{{f_0}}}\left[ {\frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$for normal adjustment
$m = \frac{{ - l}}{{{f_0}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$ for least distance of clear vision
For both cases $m\,\alpha \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}$
so $m$ decreases with increase in ${f_0}.$
The magnifying power of a telescope is
$m = \frac{{ - {f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}$ for normal adjustment
$m = \frac{{ - {f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{{{f_e}}}{D}} \right]$for least distance of clear vision
$m\,\alpha {f_0}$
So $m$ increases with increase in ${f_0}.$

options.text: Microscope will decrease but that of telescope will increase
Microscope and telescope both will decrease
Microscope and telescope both will increase
Microscope will increase but that of telescope decreases

explanation:

question: The magnifying power of a telescope is high, if its objective and eyepiece have respectively
explanation:

$m = - \frac{{{f_o}}}{{{f_e}}}$
Therefore, the magnifying power will be high when objective lens has large focal length and eye - piece has small focal length.

question: There are four convex lens ${L_1},{L_2},{L_3},{L_4}$ of focal lengths 2, 4, 6 and 8cm respectively. Two of these lenses from a telescope of length 10cm and magnifying power 4. The objective and eye lenses are respectively
explanation:
Let us assume , the focal length of eye piece and objective lens of telescope be ${f_e}$ and ${f_o}$ respectively,
The magnifying power of telescope,
$M = \frac{{{f_o}}}{{{f_e}}} = 4$
$\therefore {f_o} = 4{f_e}$ (1)
The length of telescope is given by
$L = {f_o} + {f_e}$
$10 = {f_o} + {f_e}$
$10 = 4{f_e} + {f_e}$[$\because {f_o} = 4{f_e}$ from eq.(1)]
$5{f_e} = 10$
${f_e} = 2\,cm$
and ${f_0} = 4 \times 2\,cm = 8\,cm$
Hence ${L_4}$ is the objective lens and ${L_1}$ is the eye lens
subject: Physics

question: A simple microscope consists of a concave lens of power $ - 10\,D$ and a convex lens power  $ + \,20\,D$ in contact. If the image is formed at infinity, then the magnifying power (where  $D = 20\,cm$) is

For image at infinity, $m = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{{20}}{{10}} = 2.0$

question: The focal lengths of the objective and of the eyepiece of a compound microscope are ${f_0}$ and ${f_e}$ respectively. If  $L$ is the tube length and  $D$, the least distance of distinct vision, then its angular magnification, when the image is formed at least distance of clear vision is
explanation:
Magnification of a compound microscope is
$m = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}}\left[ {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{f_0}}}$
$\frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}} = 1 - \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{f_0}}} = 1 - \frac{L}{{{f_0}}}\,(L \approx {v_0})$
$m = \left( {1 - \frac{L}{{{f_0}}}} \right)\left[ {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: The focal lengths of the objective and eyepiece of a  compound microscope are $2\,cm$ and  $3\,cm$ respectively.The distance between the objective  and eyepiece is  $15\,cm$. The final image formed is at infinity. The distances in $cm$ of object and image from objective are
explanation:
Given that
${f_0} = 2{\mkern 1mu} cm,\,{f_e} = 3{\mkern 1mu} cm\,\& L = 15{\mkern 1mu} cm$
As the final image is formed at infinity
${v_0} = L - {f_e} = 12\,cm$
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{12}} - \frac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {u_0} = 2.4\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The focal lengths of the objective and eyepiece of a telescope are $60\,cm$ and  $5\,cm$ respectively. The telescope is focused on an object $360\,cm$ from the objective and the final image is formed  at a distance of $30\,cm$ from the eye of the observer. The length of the telescope is
explanation:
Given that
${f_0} = 60{\mkern 1mu} cm,{\mkern 1mu} {f_e} = 5{\mkern 1mu} cm,{\mkern 1mu} {u_0} = - 360{\mkern 1mu} cm\,\& \,{v_e} = 30{\mkern 1mu} cm$
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}\,$
$\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} + \frac{1}{{360}} = \frac{1}{{60}}$$ \Rightarrow {v_0} = 72\,cm$
$\frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_e}}}\,\,$$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{ - 30}} - \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{5}$
$ \Rightarrow {u_e} = \frac{{ - 30}}{7}$$ \Rightarrow L = {v_0} + \left| {{u_e}} \right|$$ = 76.3\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The diameter of moon is  $3.5 \times {10^3}\,km$ and its distance from the earth is  $3.8 \times {10^5}km.$ The focal length of the objective and eyepiece are $4\,m$ and  $10\,cm$ respectively. The limit of resolution of diameter of the image of the moon will be approximately
explanation:
The angle subtend by the diameter of the moon on the unaided eye is $\alpha $
$\alpha = \frac{{3.5 \times {{10}^3}}}{{3.8 \times {{10}^5}}} \times \frac{{{{180}^0}}}{\pi }$
$ = \frac{{3.5 \times 180 \times 7}}{{38 \times 100 \times 22}}$
The magnification of telescope is
$m = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{400}}{{10}} = 40 = \frac{\beta }{\alpha }$
$\therefore $ Limit of resolution,
$\beta = \frac{{40 \times 35 \times 180 \times 7}}{{38 \times 100 \times 22}} = 21^\circ $
subject: Physics

question: A giant telescope in an observatory has objective of focal length $19\,cm$ and an eyepiece of focal length $1.0\,cm$. In normal adjustment, the telescope is used to view the moon. Diameter of image of the moon formed by the objective is (The diameter of the moon is $3.5 \times {10^6}m$and the radius of the lunar orbit round the earth is $3.8 \times {10^8}m$ )
explanation:
The diameter of the image formed by the objective lens is
$\tan \theta = \frac{{{D_1}/2}}{{\left| {{u_0}} \right|}} = \frac{{{D_1}/2}}{{{f_0}}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949819/preptracker/questions/q_31040_explanation_1764949819517.png ${D_2} = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{\left| {{u_0}} \right|}}{D_1} = \frac{{19m \times 3.5 \times {{10}^6}}}{{3.8 \times {{10}^8}}} = 17.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: If a ray of light passes through an equilateral prism such that the angle of incidence and the angle of emergence are both equal to $70 \%$ of the angle of the prism, then the angle of minimum deviation is
explanation:
using $A+\delta=i+e$
$\Rightarrow \delta=i+e-A$
Equilateral prism so $A=60^{\circ}$
And $i=e=60^{\circ} \times \dfrac{70}{100}=42^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \delta_{\text {min }}=42^{\circ}+42^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=24^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question: A small angled prism is made of a material of refractive index $\dfrac{3}{2}$. The ratio of the angles of minimum deviations when the prism is placed in air and in water of refractive index $\dfrac{4}{3}$ is
explanation:
Deviation produced by a thin prism
$\delta_{\text {min }}=A(\mu-1)$
In air, $\delta_{1}=A\left(\mu_{g}-1\right)=A\left(\dfrac{3}{2}-1\right)=\dfrac{A}{2}$
In water, ${ }_{w} \mu_{g}=\dfrac{{ }_{a} \mu_{g}}{{ }_{a} \mu_{\omega}}=\dfrac{3 / 2}{4 / 3}=\dfrac{9}{8}$
$\delta_{2}=A\left(\mu_{2}-1\right)=A\left(\dfrac{9}{8}-1\right)=\dfrac{A}{8}$
$\dfrac{\delta_{1}}{\delta_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{A}{2}}{\dfrac{A}{8}}=\dfrac{8}{2}=\dfrac{4}{1}$
subject: Physics

question: The refractive index of the material of a small angled prism is 1.6. If the angle of minimum deviation is $4.2^{\circ}$, the angle of the prism is
explanation:
$\mu=1.6$,
$\delta_{\text {min }}=4.2^{\circ}$
For small angled prism,
$\delta_{\text {min }}=(\mu-1) A \Rightarrow 4.2=(1.6-1) A$
$\therefore A=7^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question: Match the terms related to refraction through a prism (Column I) with their corresponding descriptions or conditions (Column II). Choose the correct option from the given codes.
explanation:
(A) The angle of deviation ( $\delta$ ) is given by the equation $\delta=i+e-A$ (angle of the prism), showing that it is the sum of deviations at the two faces of the prism.
(B) The angle of refraction at the first face $({r_1})$ depends on the angle of incidence $\sin r_{1}=\sin i / n_{21}$.
(C) At the minimum deviation ($\delta_{m}$), the condition, $i = e$ is satisfied and if the cross - section of the prism is an isosceles triangle, the refracted ray becomes parallel to the base.
(D) The refractive index of the prism material $\left(n_{21}\right)$ is determined using the angle A and $\delta_{m}$, as shown by the equation $n_{21}=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$. So, correct option is (3)

A - Q,       	B - S,	C - P, 	D - R

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A beam of white light is split into its constituent colors upon passing through a prism.
The refractive index of a prism for red light is greater than that for blue light.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as a prism separates white light into its constituent spectrum of colors, a process known as dispersion. Statement B is false because the refractive index for red light is actually less than that for blue light, which is why blue light bends more than red light in a prism. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The dispersion of white light by a prism occurs due to the different speeds of different colors of light in the prism material.
Red light bends the most when passing through a prism because it has the longest wavelength.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because the dispersion occurs due to the variation in speed of different colors of light within the prism material; each wavelength travels at a different speed and thus bends by a different amount. Statement B is false; in fact, violet light bends the most when passing through a prism, not red light, due to its shorter wavelength and higher refractive index. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
In a prism, the angle of minimum deviation is always equal to the angle of the prism.
The angle of minimum deviation is dependent on the wavelength of light passing through the prism.
explanation: Statement   A  is false because the angle of minimum deviation is not always equal to the angle of the prism; it depends on the refractive index of the material of the prism and the wavelength of light. Statement B is true, as the angle of minimum deviation depends on the wavelength due to the dispersion of light; different wavelengths are refracted differently, leading to different angles of deviation. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The refractive index of a prism is the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of minimum deviation.
For a prism, the refractive index can be calculated using the angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation.
explanation: Statement  A  is false; the refractive index is not calculated with the angle of incidence and the angle of minimum deviation, but rather it is based on the angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation as given by the formula  $n=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$, where $n$ is the refractive  index,  A  is the angle of the prism, and $\delta_{m}$ is the angle of minimum deviation. Statement B is true and correctly reflects the method for calculating the refractive index of the prism material. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: For a prism, the minimum angle of deviation occurs when the incident ray and the emergent ray are symmetrical with respect to the prism base.

Reason: At the position of minimum deviation, the light ray inside the prism is parallel to the base of the prism.
explanation: The assertion is true as the condition for minimum deviation is when the incident and emergent rays are symmetrical. The reason given is a correct explanation as this symmetry implies that the light ray inside the prism would be parallel to the base, satisfying the condition for minimum deviation. Correct option is (1)

Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: The angles of incidence and emergence of a light ray passing through a prism of angle $A$ are $i$ and $e$ respectively. The total deviation produced by the prism is
explanation:
Given, Angle of incidence $=i$
Angle of emergence $=e$
Angle of prism $=A$
Let angle of deviation $=\delta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952886/preptracker/questions/q_112809_explanation_1764952885623.png $P Q=$ incident ray
$R S=$ emergent ray
Angle of deviation $=$ the angle between the direction of incident ray and the emergent ray.
$\delta=\angle U T S=\angle T Q R+\angle T R Q[$ From $\triangle T Q R]$
$\delta=(\angle T Q V-\angle R Q V)+(\angle T R V-\angle Q R V)$
$ = (i - r) + \left( {e - r'} \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Now sum of angle of quadrilateral
$A Q V R=360^{\circ}$
$\angle A Q V=\angle A R V=90^{\circ}$
So, $\angle A+\angle Q V R=180^{\circ}$
From $\triangle Q R V$
$r+r^{\prime \prime}+\angle Q V R=180^{\circ}$
$r + r' = A\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eq. (1)
$\delta = i + e - \left( {r + r'} \right)\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
Put the value of ( $r+r^{\prime}$ ) in Eq. (2)
$\delta=i+e-A$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

explanation:

question: A prism is made of a glass having refractive index $\sqrt{2}$. If the angle of minimum deviation is equal to angle of the prism, then the angle of prism is
explanation:
Let $\delta_{m}$ be the angle of minimum deviation. As we know that,
$n=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}$
Given, $n=\sqrt{2}$ and $A=\delta_{m}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+A}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}=\dfrac{\sin A}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{2 \sin \dfrac{A}{2} \cos \dfrac{A}{2}}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \dfrac{A}{2}=\sqrt{2}$
$\cos \dfrac{A}{2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$[\because \sin 2 A=2 \sin A \cos A]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A}{2}=45^{\circ} \Rightarrow A=90^{\circ}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A thin glass prism of angle $9^{\circ}$ with refractive index 1.4 is combined with another glass prism of refractive index 1.6 as shown in the figure. The combination of the prism provides dispersion without deviation. Determine the angle $(A)$ of the second prism.
explanation:
For dispersion without deviation through a prism combination,
$\delta_{m_{1}}=\delta_{m_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad A_{1}\left(n_{1}-1\right)=A_{2}\left(n_{2}-1\right)$
$\Rightarrow A_{2}=A_{1} \dfrac{\left(n_{1}-1\right)}{n\left(n_{2}-1\right)}$
Here, $A_{1}=9^{\circ}, n_{1}=1.4$ and $n_{2}=1.6$
$\therefore A=A_{2}=9^{\circ} \times \dfrac{(1.4-1)}{(1.6-1)}=9^{\circ} \times \dfrac{0.4}{0.6}=6^{\circ}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A thin prism of angle $6^{\circ}$ made of glass of refractive index 1.5 is combined with another prism made of refractive index 1.75 to produce dispersion without deviation. Then, the angle of the second prism is
explanation:
For dispersion without deviation
net deviation $=0$
$\delta-\delta^{\prime}=0 \Rightarrow A(n-1)-A^{\prime}\left(n^{\prime}-1\right)=0$
where, $\delta=$ deviation by first prism
$\delta^{\prime}=$ deviation by second prism
$A=$ angle of first prism $=6^{\circ}$
$A^{\prime}=$ angle of second prism = ?
$n=$ refractive index of first prism $=1.5$
$n^{\prime}=$ refractive index of second prism $=1.75$
$\Rightarrow A^{\prime}\left(n^{\prime}-1\right)=A(n-1)$
$A' = \frac{{A(n - 1)}}{{\left( {n' - 1} \right)}} = \frac{{6(1.5 - 1)}}{{(1.75 - 1)}}$ $ = \frac{{6(0.5)}}{{(0.75)}} = 6 \times \frac{2}{3} = 4^\circ $
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Consider a glass prism  immersed  in a liquid as shown below. The refractive index of glass and liquid is 1.5 and 1.2, respectively. A ray of light enters the prism perpendicular to the face $A B$. The largest value of angle $\theta$ is, if the ray is totally reflected at the face $A C$, then
explanation:
From the ray diagram, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958165/preptracker/questions/q_112781_explanation_1764958164533.png $\alpha = 90^\circ - \theta $
Also, ${ }_{l} n_{g}=\dfrac{n_{g}}{n_{l}}=\dfrac{1.5}{1.2}=\dfrac{5}{4}$
The incident will be totally reflected if
$\sin \alpha \geq \dfrac{1}{{ }_{l} n_{g}} \Rightarrow \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right) \geq \dfrac{1}{(5 / 4)}$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta \geqslant 0.8 \Rightarrow \theta \leqslant {\cos ^{ - 1}}(0.8)$
$\therefore \quad \theta_{\text {max }}=\cos ^{-1}(0.8)$.
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: If $r$ and $r^{\prime}$ denote the angles inside the prism having angle of prism $50^{\circ}$ considering that during interval of time from $t=0$ to $t=t, r$ varies with time as $r=10^{\circ}+t^{2}$. During this time $r^{\prime}$ will vary with time as
explanation:
For refraction through a prism, As $r+r^{\prime}=A$
$r+r^{\prime}=50^{\circ}$
$10^{\circ}+t^{2}+r^{\prime}=50^{\circ}$
$r^{\prime}=40^{\circ}-t^{2}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement -A and Statement -B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
When the white light passes through a prism, the red light bends lesser than yellow and violet.
The refractive indices are different for different wavelengths in dispersive medium.
explanation:
$\delta=A(\mu-1) \Rightarrow \delta \propto \mu \propto \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$
Red light bends lesser as it has maximum wavelength. On dispersion, the light rays with longer wavelengths bends less than the ones with shorter wavelengths. Refractive index of medium is different for different wavelengths. So the correct option is (3).

options.text: Statement A is  correct but Statement B 	 incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is 	 correct.

subject: Physics

question: As shown in figure, light is incident normally on one face of the prism. A liquid of refractive index ${\mu}$ is placed on the horizontal face ${A C}$. The refractive index of prism is ${3 / 2}$. If total internal reflection taken place on face ${{AC}, m}$ should be less than ${\dfrac{I \sqrt{3}}{4}}$, where ${I}$ is an integer. Find the valueof ${I}$.
explanation:
Critical angle between glass and liquid.
$\sin \theta_{C}=\dfrac{\mu}{(3 / 2)}=\dfrac{2 \mu}{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951155/preptracker/questions/q_33692_explanation_1764951155128.png Angle of incidence on $A C=60^{\circ}$
For $TIR,$ $i>\theta_{C} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{3} \mu$
$\mu < \dfrac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}=\dfrac{I \sqrt{3}}{4} \quad \text { (given) } \quad \text { So, } I=3$
subject: Physics

question: A parallel glass slab of refractive index ${\sqrt{3}}$ is placed in contact with an equilateral prism of refractive index ${\sqrt{2}}$. A ray is incident on left surface of slab as shown. The slab and prism combination is surrounded by air. Find the magnitude of minimum possible deviation  of this ray by slab-prism combination.
explanation:
The slab does not object for minimum deviation by prism, thus for minimum deviation, we use ${r_{1}=r_{2}=30^{\circ}}$ as shown in figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951130/preptracker/questions/q_33598_explanation_1764951130004.png ${\Rightarrow \sin i=\sqrt{2} \sin 30^{\circ}}$ or ${i=45^{\circ}}$
Thus minimum deviation ${=2 i-A=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ}}$
${\Rightarrow \sin i=\sqrt{2} \sin 30^{\circ}}$ or ${i=45^{\circ}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Refraction through a Prism

question: The cross-section of a prism has the form of an isosceles triangle. One of the equal faces is silvered. A ray is normally incident on the other once's face and being reflected twice inside the prism emerges through the base of prism perpendicular to base. Angle of prism is found to be $\pi /n.$ The value of $n$ is
explanation:
Let angle of prism is ${\alpha}$ and other two angles are ${\beta}$ each. As ${N N^{\prime}}$ is parallel to ${P Q}$.
${\angle P Q R=\angle Q R N^{\prime}}$ (Complimentary angles)
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \alpha=\beta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951125/preptracker/questions/q_33578_explanation_1764951125422.png Now from $\vartriangle ABC\,\,\alpha + 2\beta = 180^\circ $
${\alpha+2(2 \alpha)=180^{\circ}}$
${5 \alpha=180^{\circ}}$
${\alpha=36^{\circ}=\dfrac{\pi}{5}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Prism with One of the Surface is Silvered

question:
A horizontal ray is incident on one of the refracting faces of a prism of angle $8^{\circ}$. The ray strikes a vertical plane mirror after refraction as shown in figure. Find the angle by which the mirror should be rotated in order to make the reflected ray antiparallel to incident ray.
(Take $R.I.$ of material of prism $=5 / 4$ )
explanation:
For thin prism,
$\delta = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)A = \left( {\frac{5}{4} - 1} \right) \times 8 = \frac{8}{4} = 2^\circ $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951117/preptracker/questions/q_33483_explanation_1764951117191.png By geometry of the figure,
angle through which the mirror should be rotated in order to make the ray horizontal is $\theta=1^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question: For a prism of refractive index 1.732, the angle of minimum deviation is equal to the angle of the prism. The angle of the prism is
explanation:
Given, refractive index of prism
${\mu=1.732, \delta_{m}={A}}$
${\therefore 1.732=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}}$
${\sqrt{3}=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{2 A}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}=\dfrac{\sin A}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}}$
${\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\dfrac{2 \sin \dfrac{A}{2} \cos \dfrac{A}{2}}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=2 \times \cos \dfrac{A}{2}}$
${\Rightarrow \cos \dfrac{A}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}$
$\dfrac{A}{2}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=30^{\circ}$
$A=30^{\circ} \times 2=60^{\circ}$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: The angle of prism is ${5^{\circ}}$ and its refractive indices for red and violet colours are 1.5 and 1.6 respectively. The angular dispersion produced by the prism is
explanation:
Given, ${\mu_{R}=1.5, \mu_{V}=1.6, A=5^{\circ}}$
Angular dispersion ${\omega=\left(\mu_{V}-\mu_{R}\right) A}$$=(1.6-1.5) \times 5^{\circ}=0.1 \times 5^{\circ}$
${\omega=0.5^{\circ}}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: The refractive index of the prism is ${\cot \left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$ and the refractive angle of the prism is ${A}$.The angle of minimum deviation is
explanation:
Given, ${\mu=\cot \dfrac{A}{2}}$
Refractive angle ${=A}$
${\therefore \mu=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}}$
${\Rightarrow \sin \dfrac{A}{2} \times \cot \dfrac{A}{2}=\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \cos \dfrac{A}{2}=\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\dfrac{A}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \delta_{m}=\pi-2 A=180^{\circ}-2 A}$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A refractive index of material of a prism is 2. What is the maximum possible refraction angle of the prism, so that light emerges from the prism
explanation:
Given, refractive index, ${\mu=2, A=}$ ?
If ${C}$ is the critical angle for prism, then
${\mu=\dfrac{1}{\sin C} \quad \therefore \quad \sin C=\dfrac{1}{\mu}=\dfrac{1}{2}=\sin 30^{\circ}}$
or ${C=30^{\circ}}$
When ${A>2 C}$, the ray does not emerge from prism. So the maximum refracting angle of prism, ${A=2 C=60^{\circ}}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: The separation of a beam of light into its constituent wavelengths is called
explanation:
The splitting of white light into its constituent colours of different wavelengths is called dispersion.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A ray falls on a prism $ABC\,(AB = BC)$ and travels as shown in figure. The least value of refractive index of material of the prism, should be
explanation:
The given prism is a right angled prism and angle $\angle L M N=45^{\circ}$. Since, the ray is suffering total internal reflection and the critical angle is the angle of incidence in the denser medium for which the angle of redfraction in the rarer medium is $90^{\circ}$, $i.e.$ $C=45^{\circ}$ and
$\mu = \frac{1}{{\sin {\kern 1pt} C}} = \frac{1}{{\sin \,{{45}^ \circ }}} = \sqrt {2.} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956808/preptracker/questions/q_33311_explanation_1764956807647.png
subject: Physics

question: A prism of crown glass with refracting angle of $5^{\circ}$ and mean refractive index $=1.51$ is combined with a flint glass prism of refractive index $=1.65$ to produce no deviation. Find the angle of flint glass.
explanation:
Let $A^{\prime}$ be the angle of flint glass prism.
Here, $A=5^{\circ}$ and $\mu=1.51$ for crown
glass prism.
Deviation produced by flint glass,
$\delta^{\prime}=(\mu-1) A=(1.51-1) \times 5=2.55^{\circ}$
For no deviation, $\delta^{\prime}=\delta$
or $\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,0.65{A^\prime } = 2.55$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{A^\prime } = \frac{{2.55}}{{0.65}} = {3.92^ \circ }$
subject: Physics

question: If minimum deviation $=30^{\circ}$, then speed of light in shown prism will be
subject: Physics

question: Which one of the following is not associated with the total internal reflection?
explanation: Dispersion of light is not associated with total internal reflection.
subject: Physics

question: One of the refracting surface of a prism is silvered. A ray of light incident at an angle ${60^{\circ}}$ retraces its path. If the refractive index of the material of prism is ${\sqrt{3}}$, the angle of prism will be
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950022/preptracker/questions/q_33132_explanation_1764950021774.png ${\dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin r}=\sqrt{3}}$
${\sin r=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow r=30^{\circ}}$
In ${\triangle A B C}$
${A+90^{\circ}+\left(90-30^{\circ}\right)=180}$
${A+90^{\circ}+60^{\circ}=180^{\circ}}$
${A=30^{\circ}}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Prism with One of the Surface is Silvered

question: A ray of light enters into the prism normally with the face 1 and comes out normally with the face 3. The minimum refractive index of the prism is
explanation:
Snell's law at 2 , for grazing of the ray:
${\sin \theta=\dfrac{1}{n}}$ or ${n=\operatorname{cosec} \theta}$
subject: Physics

question: A ray of light makes normal incidence on the diagonal face of a right-angled prism as shown in figure such that the ray strikes the surface ${A B}$ of the prism after entering the prism. If ${\theta=37^{\circ}}$ then the angle of deviation is ${\left(\sin 37^{\circ}=3 / 5\right)}$
explanation:
${\theta_{c}=\sin ^{-1} \dfrac{3}{5}=37^{\circ}}$
From figure,
${\delta=74^{\circ}+53^{\circ}}$ [By exterior angle theory]
${\Rightarrow \delta=127^{\circ}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950016/preptracker/questions/q_33107_explanation_1764950015518.png
subject: Physics

question: A light ray enters through a right angled prism at point ${P}$ with the angle of incidence ${30^{\circ}}$ as shown in figure. It travels through the prism parallel to its base ${B C}$ and emerges along the face ${A C}$. The refractive index of the prism is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949985/preptracker/questions/q_33013_explanation_1764949984751.png $A = 90^\circ $
${\rm{In}}\,\,{\rm{prism}},A + 90 - {r_1} + 90 - {r_2} = 180$
$ \Rightarrow {r_1} + {r_2} = A$
${r_1} = 90^\circ - {r_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Applying Snell’s law at second refracting surface
$\mu \,\sin \,{r_2} = 1 \times \sin \,90^\circ $
$\sin \,{r_2} = \frac{1}{\mu }.$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \,{r_2} = \frac{{\sqrt {{\mu ^2} - 1} }}{\mu }$
$ \Rightarrow $ Apply Snell’s law, on first surface
$1 \cdot \sin 30^\circ = \mu \sin \left( {{r_1}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 1 \times \frac{1}{2} = \mu \times \sin \left( {90^\circ - \,{r_2}} \right)$
$\frac{1}{2} = \mu \cos \,{r_2} = \mu \times \frac{{\sqrt {{\mu ^2} - 1} }}{\mu }$
On squaring $\frac{1}{4} = {\mu ^2} - 1$
$ \Rightarrow {\mu ^2} = \frac{5}{4} \Rightarrow \mu = \frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: If the angle of minimum deviation is equal to angle of a prism for an equilateral prism, then the speed of light inside the prism is ____ .
explanation:
For minimum angle of deviation, refractive index of a prism is given by
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+d_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$
${A=d_{m}=60^{\circ}}$
${\mu=\dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{3}}$
${\dfrac{c}{v}=\mu=\sqrt{3}}$
${v=\dfrac{c}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3} \times 10^{8} {~m} / {s}}$.
subject: Physics

question: A thin prism $P_{1}$ with an angle $6^{\circ}$ and made of glass of refractive index 1.54 is combined with another prism $P_{2}$ made from glass of refractive index 1.72 to produce dispersion without average deviation. The angle of prism $P_{2}$ is
explanation:
For direct vision combination, the angle of deviations of both are equal, so, $\delta_{1}=\delta_{2}$.
$\Rightarrow\left(\mu_{1}-1\right) A=\left(\mu_{2}-1\right) B$.
Where $A$ be the angle of prism $P_{1}$. and $B$ is the angle of prism $P_{2}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955602/preptracker/questions/q_32936_explanation_1764955601620.png $A(1.54-1)=B(1.72-1) ;$
$6^{\circ}(1.54-1)=B(1.72-1)$
$\dfrac{6^{\circ}(0.54)}{0.72}=B$ or $B=4.5^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When white light falls on the compact disc (CD), multicolours are seen after reflection.

Reason: CD disc behaves like a prism.
explanation: $\mathrm{CD}$ acts as a diffraction grating, dispersing the light into its constituent colors. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A total reflecting prism is used to erect the inverted image without deviation.

Reason: Rays of light incident parallel to base of prism emerge out as parallel rays.
explanation: Light entering the prism refracts and undergoes internal reflection, leading to emergence parallel to the base. This results in inverted rays due to the refraction within the prism. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: If the angles of the base of the prism are equal. then in the position of minimum deviation. the refracted ray will pass parallel to the base of prism.

Reason: In the case of minimum deviation the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of emergence.
explanation: When the angles of the base of the prism are equal, in the position of minimum deviation, the refracted ray passes parallel to the base. So, the angle of incidence equals the angle of emergence $(i=e)$. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: The ratio of angle of minimum deviation of a prism in air and when dipped in water will be $\left({ }_{a} \mu_{g}=3 / 2\right.$ and $\left.{ }_{a} \mu_{w}=4 / 3\right)$ if prism angle is very small.
explanation: $\dfrac{\delta_{w}}{\delta_{a}}=\dfrac{\left({ }_{w} \mu_{g}-1\right)}{\left({ }_{a} \mu_{g}-1\right)}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{9}{8}-1\right)}{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}-1\right)}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: For the ray diagram shown in the adjoining figure, the false statement is:
explanation: $\mu=\dfrac{\sin i_{1}}{\sin r_{1}}=\dfrac{\sin i_{2}}{\sin r_{2}}$.
subject: Physics

question: The angle of a glass prism is $4.5^{\circ}$ and its refractive index is 1.52 . The angle of minimum deviation will be-
subject: Physics

question: A ray of light is incident at $60^{\circ}$ on one face of a prism of angle $30^{\circ}$ and the emergent ray makes $30^{\circ}$ with the incident ray. The refractive index of the prism is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949967/preptracker/questions/q_32706_explanation_1764949966793.png $i=60^{\circ}, A=30^{\circ}, \delta=30^{\circ}$
As we known
$i+e=A+\delta$
$e=A+\delta-i$
$=A+\delta-i$
$=30^{\circ}+30^{\circ}-60^{\circ}$
$e=0^{\circ}, r_{2}=0^{\circ}$
As, $r_{1}+r_{2}=A$
$\therefore r_{1}=30^{\circ}$
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin i}{\sin r} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\sqrt{3}=1.732$
$\mu=1.732$.
subject: Physics

question: When light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on an equilateral prism kept in its minimum deviation position, it is found that the angle of deviation equals the angle of the prism itself. The refractive index of the material of the prism with respect to the wavelength $\lambda$ is
explanation:
According to the relation
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{A+\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{60^{\circ}+60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2}}$
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{1}=\sqrt{3}$
$\mu=\sqrt{3}$.
subject: Physics

question: A ray of light undergoes refraction through right angled isosceles prism of $\mu>\sqrt{2}$. Then which of the following shows a deviation of $90^{\circ}$.
explanation:
$\mu>\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\mu} < \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \sin C < \sin 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow C < 45^{\circ}$
Hence a ray striking a glass-air surface from inside the prism will be reflected back if angle of incidence is not less than $45^{\circ}$.
$90^{\circ}$ deviation is produced if the ray strikes on one of the sides normally as shown.
Here, $I=45^{\circ}$ and hence reflection takes place.
subject: Physics

question: The angle of minimum deviation measured with a prism is $30^{\circ}$ and the angle of prism is $60^{\circ}$. The refractive index of prism material is-
explanation:
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{\delta_{\text {min }}+A}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{A}{2}}$
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin \left(\dfrac{30+60}{2}\right)}{\sin \dfrac{60}{2}}$
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 35^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: The refractive index of the material of prism of $60^{\circ}$ angle is $\sqrt{2}$. At what angle the ray of light be incident on it so that minimum deviation takes place?
explanation:
$i=e \quad r_{1}=r_{2}=\dfrac{A}{2}=30^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}\sin i & =\sqrt{2} \sin 30^{\circ} \\i & =45^{\circ} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Deviation of $5^{\circ}$ is observed from a prism whose angle is small and whose refractive index is 1.5 . The angle of prism is
explanation:
According to the relation
$\delta=(\mu-1) A \Rightarrow 5=(\mu-1) A$
$5=(1.5-1) A$
$A=10^{\circ}$.
subject: Physics

question: A ray $P Q$ incident on the refracting face $B A$ is refracted in the prism $B A C$ as shown in the figure and emerges from the other refracting face $A C$ as $R S$, such that $A Q=A R$. If the angle of prism $A=60^{\circ}$ and the refractive index of the material of prism is $\sqrt{3}$, then the angle of deviation of the ray is:
explanation:
For a given prism, the angle of deviation depends upon the angle of incidence of the light rays falling on the prism. Taking triangle $D B C$, we have $\delta=\angle D B C+\angle D C B$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956795/preptracker/questions/q_32682_explanation_1764956794753.png Since, $\triangle P B C$ is an equilateral triangle, therefore
$\angle D B C=\dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2}=30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \delta=30^{\circ}+30^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$.
Hence, angle of deviation of the ray is $60^{\circ}$.
subject: Physics

question: If one face of prism is silvered having prism angle $30^{\circ}$ and $\mu=\sqrt{2}$. What will be the angle of incidence, so that the incident ray retraces its path?
explanation:
$A=30^{\circ} \quad \mu=1.414=\sqrt{2} \quad i_{2}=0^{\circ}$
$r_{2}=0^{\circ}$ (as the refracted ray retraces its path)
$r_{1}+r_{2}=A$
$r_{1}=A=30^{\circ}$
$\mu=\dfrac{\sin i_{1}}{\sin r_{1}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{\sin i_{1}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow \sin i_{1}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow i_{1}=45^{\circ}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Prism with One of the Surface is Silvered

question: A thin prism $P_{1}$ of angle $4^{\circ}$, are made from a glass of refractive index 1.54, is combined with another thin prism $P_{2}$ made from a glass of refractive index 1.72 , to produce dispersion without deviation. The angle of $P_{2}$ is:
explanation:
For no deviation, $A_{1}\left(\mu_{1}-1\right)=A_{2}\left(\mu_{2}-1\right)$
$\Rightarrow A_{2}=\left(4^{\circ}\right)\left(\dfrac{1.54-1}{1.72-1}\right)=3^{\circ}$.
subject: Physics

question:
The refracting angle of a glass prism is $30^{\circ}$. A ray is incident on one of the faces perpendicular to it. The angle of deviation $\delta$ between the incident ray and that leaves the prism is
$($ Refractive index of glass $=1.5)$
$\left(\sin 30^{\circ}=0.5, \sin (48.6)^{\circ}=0.75\right)$
explanation:
Given, $A=30^{\circ}, m=1.5 ; i_{1}=0^{\circ}$
In a prism, ${r_1} + {r_2} = A\,$
$\therefore \,{r_2} = A = 30^\circ $
from snell's law, $\mu=\dfrac{\sin i_{2}}{\sin r_{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow 1.5 = \frac{{\sin {i_2}}}{{\sin 30^\circ {\rm{ }}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \sin {i_2} = 1.5\sin 30^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow \sin {i_2} = 1.5 \times \frac{1}{2} = 0.75$
$ \Rightarrow {i_2} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}(0.75) = 48.6^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow $ Angle of Deviation is given by
$\delta=\left(i_{1}+i_{2}\right)-A=\left(0+48.6^{\circ}\right)-30^{\circ}=18.6^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question: A given ray of light suffers minimum deviation in an equilateral prism $P$. Additional prism $Q$ and $R$ of identical shape and of the same material as $P$ are now added as shown in the figure. The ray will now suffer
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956803/preptracker/questions/q_32520_explanation_1764956802629.jpg  There will be no refraction from $P$ to $Q$ and then from $Q$ to $R$ (all beign identical). Hence the ray will now have the same deviation as net deviation caused by $Q$ and $R$ will be zero cancelling each others.
subject: Physics

question: A hollow prism is filled with water and placed in air. It will deviate the incident rays
explanation: The propagation of light ray through a prism is shown below   https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949951/preptracker/questions/q_32504_explanation_1764949951455.png  When a light ray travel from air (rarer) to prism filled with water (denser) of higher redfractive index, then it bends towards the normal inside it. At other fae, the light is travelling from denser to rarer medium, so it bends away from normal. Thus, the prsim deviates the incident ray towards the base of prism as shown in figure above.

options.text: Towards or away from base depending on the location

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: There is no dispersion of light refracted through a rectangular glass slab.

Reason: Dispersion of light is the phenomenon of splitting of a beam of white light into its constituent colours.
explanation:
There is no dispersion of light refracted through a rectangular glass slab. This is correct. When light passes through a rectangular glass slab, it undergoes refraction but does not disperse into its constituent colors. Instead, it remains as a single beam.
Dispersion of light as the phenomenon of splitting a beam of white light into its constituent colors. While this is an accurate definition of dispersion, it doesn't explain why there is no dispersion in the case of a rectangular glass slab. The absence of dispersion in a rectangular glass slab is due to the fact that the different colors of light experience the same amount of deviation when passing through the slab, so they emerge parallel to each other and are not separated.
So, both the Assertion and Reason are correct, but the Reason does not provide the correct explanation for the phenomenon described in the Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A beam of light consisting of red, green and blue colours is incident on a right angled prism as shown in figure. The refractive indices of the material of prism for red, green and blue colours respectively are $1.33,1.44,1.47$. The prism will
explanation:
For $i>i_{C}$, as all rays incident at $45^{\circ}$ on face $A C$.
${\rm{So}},\sin \,\,45^\circ > \sin {i_C}$
$ \Rightarrow \sin \,\,45^\circ > \frac{1}{\mu } \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} > \frac{1}{\mu } \Rightarrow \mu > \sqrt 2 $
${\rm{or}}\,\,\mu > 1.41$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956804/preptracker/questions/q_32491_explanation_1764956804118.png The deviation for red in comparison to other colour is less, whereas deviation for green and blue are nearly same so, prism will separate part of red colour from green and blue colours.

options.text: Separate part of blue colour from red and green colour
Separate all the three colours from one another
Separate part of the red colour from green and blue colours
Not separate even partially any colour from the other two colours.
