question:
Assertion: A bi-convex lens of focal length $10\,cm$ is split into two equals parts by a plane parallel to its principal axis. The focal length of the each part will be $20\,cm$

Reason: Focal length does not depend on the radii of curvature of two surfaces.
explanation:
A bi-convex lens with a focal length of $10\,cm$ is split into two equal parts by a plane parallel to its principal axis, and the focal length of each part will be $20\,cm$. This is incorrect. When a biconvex lens is split into two equal parts by a plane parallel to its principal axis, each part still remains bi-convex, and the focal length will not change. The focal length of each part will remain $10\,cm$, not become $20\,cm$.
Focal length does not depend on the radii of curvature of the two surfaces. This is not correct. The focal length of a lens is directly related to the radii of curvature of its surfaces. The lens formula, which is $1{\rm{/}}f = \left( {n - 1} \right)\left( {1{\rm{/}}R\,1 - 1{\rm{/}}R\,2} \right),$ clearly shows that the radii of curvature (${R\,1}$ and${R\,2}$) affect the focal length $\left( f \right)$ of the lens.
Since both the Assertion and the Reason are incorrect, the correct choice is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: A wire mesh consisting of very small squares is viewed at a distance of $8\,cm$ through a magnifying converging lens of focal legnth $10\,cm$, kept close to the eye. The magnification produced by the lens is
explanation:
Lens formula is given by
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given, $f = 10\,cm$ (as lens is converging)
$u = - 8\,cm$ (as object is placed on left side of the lens)
Substituting these values in Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{{ - 8}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{10}} - \frac{1}{8}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} = \frac{{8 - 10}}{{80}} \Rightarrow v = \frac{{80}}{{ - 2}} = - 40\,cm$
Hence, magnification produced by the lens,
$m=\dfrac{v}{u}=\dfrac{-40}{-8}=5$
Virtual erect image formed on the same side of object.
subject: Physics

question: A point object is moving at a constant speed of$1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ along the principal axis of a convex lens of focal length $10\,cm$. The speed of the image is also $1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$, when the object is at____$cm$ from the optic centre of the lens.
explanation:
Denote $v_{O L}=$ Speed of object.
$v_{I L}=$ Speed of image.
$\Rightarrow v_{i L}=\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{u^{2}}\right) v_{O L}$
$\Rightarrow 1=\dfrac{v^{2}}{u^{2}} \times 1$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=u^{2}$
We know, for lens:
$\dfrac{1}{f}=\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{u}$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{u f}{u-f}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{u f}{u-f}\right)^{2}=u^{2}$ [using equation (1)]
$ \Rightarrow u = 2f = 20\,cm$.
Correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of an Object and Image Infront of a Lens

question: An equiconvex lens made of glass of refractive index $\dfrac{3}{2}$ has focal length $f$ in air. It is completely immersed in water of refractive index $\dfrac{4}{3}$. The percentage change in the focal length is
explanation:
As same lens,
$\left[\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{R_{2}}\right]=$ constant $=K$ (say)
$\dfrac{1}{f_{a}}=\left[\mu_{a}-1\right] K$
$\dfrac{1}{f_{l}}=\left[\dfrac{\mu_{a}}{\mu_{l}}-1\right] K$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{f_{l}}{f_{a}}=\dfrac{[1.5-1]}{\left[\dfrac{1.5}{4 / 3}-1\right]}=\dfrac{0.5}{\dfrac{1.5 \times 3}{4}-1}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{f_{l}}{f_{a}}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 4}{0.5}=4$
$\Rightarrow$ Required
$\%=\dfrac{f_{l}-f_{a}}{f_{a}} \times 100=\left[\left(\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{0}}\right)-1\right] \times 100$
$=(4-1) \times 100=300 \%$
It is an "increase" as it is positive.
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Lens which is Placed in a Transparent Medium

question:
Two thin lenses are of same focal lengths $(f)$, but one is convex and the other is concave.
When they are placed in contact with each other, the equivalent focal length of the combination will be:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955555/preptracker/questions/q_32437_explanation_1764955554622.png The equivalent Power is: $P = {P_1} - {P_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{f_{eq}}}} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {f_{ev}} = \infty $
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: In the figure shown here, what is the equivalent focal length of the combination of lenses (Assume that all layers are thin)?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949948/preptracker/questions/q_32433_explanation_1764949947923.png $\frac{1}{f} = [\mu - 1]\,\,\left[ {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right]$
$\frac{1}{{{f_1}}} = [1.6 - 1]\,\left[ {\frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{1}{{20}}} \right] = \frac{{ - 3}}{{100}}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = [1.5 - 1]\,\left[ {\frac{1}{{20}} + \frac{1}{{20}}} \right] = \frac{1}{{20}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{f_3}}} = \frac{{ - 3}}{{100}}$
These lenses are in contact.
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{f_{eq}}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_3}}}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_{eq}}}} = - \frac{3}{{100}} + \frac{1}{{20}} - \frac{3}{{100}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{100}}$
$ \Rightarrow {f_{eq}} = - 100\,cm$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens produces a real image $m$  times the size of the object. What will be the object distance from the lens?
explanation:
Magnification$ = \frac{f}{{f + u}}$
$ \Rightarrow - m = \frac{f}{{f + u}} \Rightarrow u = - \left( {\frac{{m + 1}}{m}} \right)f$
subject: Physics

question: Find focal length and nature of silvered equiconvex lens of radius of curvature $20\,cm$ & refractive index 2 placed in a medium of refractive index 3.
explanation:
The effective focal length is
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_m}}} - \frac{2}{{{f_l}}}$
${f_m} - $focal length of the mirror
${f_l} - $focal length of lens https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949941/preptracker/questions/q_32395_explanation_1764949940928.png ${f_m} = R/2 = - 20/2 = - 10\,cm$
$1/{f_l} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{{{\mu _1}}} - 1} \right)[(1/{R_1}) - (1/{R_2})]$
$1/{f_l} = \left[ {(2/3) - 1} \right]\left[ {(1/20) - (1/ - 20} \right]$
$1/{f_l} = ( - 1/3)(1/10) = - 1/30$
$1/F = (1/{f_m}) - (2/{f_l})$
$1/F = ( - 1/10) - 2( - 1/30)$
$F = - 30\,cm$
The lens will be like a concave mirror of focal length$30\,cm$, i.e., nature will be converging.

options.text: $30\,cm$, silvered lens will be like a convex mirror
$30\,cm$, silvered lens will be like a concave mirror
$20\,cm$, silvered lens will be like a convex mirror
$0\,cm$, silvered lens will be like a convex mirror

subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a lens with Silvered Surface/lens and mirror combination

question: The distance between a convex lens and a plane mirror is $10\,cm$. The parallel rays incident on the convex lens after reflection from the mirror form image at the optical centre of the lens. Focal length of lens will be
explanation:
From the figure, using property of plane mirror
Image distance= Object distance
$f - 10 = 10 \Rightarrow f = 20\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a lens with Silvered Surface/lens and mirror combination

question: If ${I_1}$  and ${I_2}$ be the size of the images respectively for the two positions of lens in the displacement method, then the size of the object is given by
explanation: $\frac{{{I_1}}}{h} = \frac{v}{u}$ and $\frac{{{I_2}}}{h} = \frac{u}{v} \Rightarrow {h^2} = {I_1}{I_2}$
subject: Physics

question: A combination of two thin lenses of the same material with focal lengths ${f_1}$  and ${f_2},$ arranged on a common axis minimises chromatic aberration, if the distance between them is
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Refraction at Curved Surfaces

question: The radii of curvatures of the surfaces of the lenses are as given in the following diagrams. Which one of the following spherical lenses does not exhibit dispersion?
explanation:
For no dispersion,
$f = \infty \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f} = 0$
$\left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {R_1} = {R_2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Refraction at Curved Surfaces

question: A thin plano-convex lens acts like a concave mirror of focal length $0.2\,m\,$ when silvered from its plane surface. The refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5. The radius of curvature of the convex surface of the lens will be
explanation:
The given lens is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949937/preptracker/questions/q_32371_explanation_1764949936906.png Let $R$ is the radius of curvature of the curved surface. The effective focal length of the combination is
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_m}}} - \frac{2}{{{f_l}}}$
${f_m} = \infty ,\,F = - 20cm$ (concave mirror)
$\frac{{ - 1}}{{20}} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{{f_l}}}\quad \Rightarrow {f_l} = 40cm$
$\frac{1}{{{f_l}}} = (1.5 - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right)$
$\frac{1}{{{f_l}}} = \frac{1}{{2R}}$
$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{{f_l}}}{2} = 20\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a lens with Silvered Surface/lens and mirror combination

question: A point object is placed at distance of $20\,cm$ from a thin plano convex lens of focal length $15\,cm$. The plane surface of the lens is now silvered. The image created by the system is at
explanation:
The effective focal length is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949935/preptracker/questions/q_32367_explanation_1764949935493.png $\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_m}}} - \frac{2}{{{f_l}}}$
${f_m} = \infty $
Given that ${f_l} = 15\,cm$
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_m}}} - \frac{2}{{{f_l}}} = \frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{2}{{15}}$
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{15}}$ (concave lens)
The position of the image is
$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{F}$
$u = - 20cm,\quad F = \frac{{ - 15}}{2}cm$
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{{20}} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{15}}$
$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{15}} + \frac{1}{{20}} = \frac{{ - 8 + 3}}{{60}} = \frac{{ - 5}}{{60}}$
$v = - 12cm$

options.text: 60 $cm$ to the left of the system
60 $cm$ to the right of the system
12 $cm$ to the left of the system
12 $cm$ to the right of the system

subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a lens with Silvered Surface/lens and mirror combination

question: A student measures the focal length of a convex lens by putting an object pin at a distance ‘\[u\]’ from the lens and measuring the distance ‘\[v\]’ of the image pin. The graph between ‘\[u\]’ and ‘\[v\]’ plotted by the student should look like
explanation:
The equation of a convex lens is
$\frac{1}{v} = - \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} = - \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u}$
When $u$ = (-)ve object is real
$v$ =(+) ve image is real
When $u$ lies between $ - f$ to $ - \infty $ then $v$ is (+) ve
When $u$ lies between $ - f$ to 0 then $v$ is (-) ve
The correct graph is (2)
subject: Physics

question: The radius of curvature of the curved surface of a plano-convex lens is$20\,cm$. If the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5, it will
explanation:
Here, $R = 20\,cm,{\mkern 1mu} \mu = 1.5,$ on substituting the values
$\frac{1}{{{f_1}}} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{1}{{ - R}}} \right)$
$\frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right)$
For both cases
$f = \frac{R}{{\mu - 1}} = \frac{{20}}{{1.5 - 1}} = 40\,cm$of converging nature as $f > 0.$ Therefore, lens act as a convex lens irrespective of the side on which the object lies.

options.text: Act as a concave lens for the objects that lie on its curved side
Act as a convex lens for the objects that lie on its curved side
Act as a concave lens irrespective of side on which the object lies
Act as a convex lens irrespective of the side on   which the object lies

subject: Physics

question:
Convergent lens property of converging remains same in any medium.
Property of lens (diverging or converging) depends on the surrounding medium.
explanation: When a convergent lens is placed inside a transparent medium having refractive index greater than that of material of lens, it behaves as a divergent lens.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A double convex lens $(\mu = 1.5)$ has focal length $10\,cm$. When the lens is immersed in water $(\mu = 4/3)$ its focal length becomes $40\,cm$.

Reason: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{{{\mu _g} - {\mu _m}}}{{{\mu _m}}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
explanation:
Focal length of lens immersed in water is four times the focal length of lens in air.
$\frac{1}{{10}} = \left( {\frac{{1.5}}{1} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{{1.5}}{{\left( {4/3} \right)}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow $$\frac{f}{{10}} = 4 \Rightarrow f = 40\,cm$
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of  the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The focal length of lens does not change when red light is replaced by blue light.

Reason: The focal length of lens depend on colour of light used.
explanation:
Focal length of the lens depend upon it’s refractive index as $\frac{1}{f} \propto (\mu - 1).$
Since ${\mu _b} < {\mu _r}$ so ${f_b} > {f_r}$
Therefore, the focal length of a lens decreases when red light is replaced by blue light. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of  the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: An air bubble in a glass slab with refractive index 1.5 (near normal incidence) is ${\rm{5}}\,cm$ deep when viewed from the surface and  $3\,cm$ deep when viewed from the opposite face. The thickness (in $cm$) of the slab is.
explanation:
The apparent depth from face-1 is
$\frac{{{t_1}}}{{1.5}} = 5\, \Rightarrow {t_1} = 7.5\,cm$
The apparent depth from face-2 is
$\frac{{{t_2}}}{{1.5}} = 3\,\, \Rightarrow {t_2} = 4.5\,cm$
$\therefore \quad t = 12\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical glass $\left( {{\mu _g} = \frac{3}{2}} \right)$ equiconvex lenses of focal length f each are kept in contact. The space between the two lenses is filled with water $\left( {{\mu _w} = \frac{4}{3}} \right)$.The focal length of the combination is
explanation:
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{3}{2} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \left( { - \frac{1}{R}} \right)} \right)\, \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{R}$
$\frac{1}{{f'}} = \left( {\frac{4}{3} - 1} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R}} \right)\, \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}\left( { - \frac{2}{R}} \right) = - \frac{2}{{3R}}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_{eff}}}} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{{f'}} + \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2}{R} - \frac{2}{{3R}} = \frac{4}{{3R}}$
$\therefore {f_{eff}} = \frac{{3f}}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: Two similar thin equi-convex lenses, of focal length $f$ each, are kept coaxially in contact with each other such that the focal length of the combination is ${F_1}$. When the space between the two lenses is filled with glycerine (which has the same refractive index ($\mu  = 1.5$) as that of glass) then the equivalent focal length is ${F_2}$. The ratio ${F_1}:{F_2}$ will be :
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949924/preptracker/questions/q_32159_explanation_1764949924239.png Equivalent focal length in air $\frac{1}{{{F_1}}} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2}{f}$
When glycerin is filled inside, glycerin lens behaves like a diverging lens of focal length (–f)
$\frac{1}{{{F_2}}} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{{{F_1}}}{{{F_2}}} = \frac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens ‘$A$’ of focal length $20\,cm$ and a concave lens ‘$B$’ of focal length $5\,cm$ are kept along the same axis with a distance ‘$d$’ between them. If a parallel beam of light falling-on ‘$A$’ leaves ‘$B$’ as a parallel beam, then the distance ‘$d$’ in cm will be
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955534/preptracker/questions/q_32141_explanation_1764955534192.png For ${{\rm{L}}_{\rm{1}}}$
$\frac{1}{{{v_1}}} - \frac{1}{\infty } = \frac{1}{{20}}$
$ \Rightarrow $ ${v_1} = 20\,cm$
For ${L_2}$
$\frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{1}{{\left( {20 - d} \right)}} = \frac{1}{{ - 5}}$
$d - 20 = - 5$
$d = 20 - 5 = 15\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A bioconvex lens has radii of curvature, 20 $cm$ each. If the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5, the power of the lens is
explanation:
${R_1} = {R_2} = 20\,cm = 0.2\,m$
$\mu = \frac{3}{2}$
$P = \frac{1}{f} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
Take sign as +ve in incident ray direction https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949919/preptracker/questions/q_32137_explanation_1764949919368.png $P = \left( {\frac{3}{2} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{0.2}} + \frac{1}{{0.2}}} \right)$
$P = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{2}{{0.2}}} \right) = \frac{{10}}{2} = + 5D$
subject: Physics

question: A point object is placed at a distance of 60 $cm$ from a convex lens of focal length 30 $cm$. If a plane mirror were put perpendicular to the principal axis of the lens and at a distance of 40 $cm$ from it, the final image would be formed at a distance of
explanation:
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{{ - 60}} = \frac{1}{{30}}$
$v = 60\,cm$
The image will be virtual. The first image acts as virtual object and second image is formed at $20\,cm$ to the left of the mirror. The second image acts as object for the lens.
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{{ - 20}} = \frac{1}{{30}}$
$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{30}} - \frac{1}{{20}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{60}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{final}} = - 60\,cm$
The correct option is (3).

options.text: 30 $cm$ from the lens, it would be a real image
30 $cm$ from the plane mirror, it would be a virtual image
20 $cm$ from the plane mirror, it would be a virtual image
20 $cm$ from the lens, it would be a real image

subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a lens with Silvered Surface/lens and mirror combination

question: A parallel beam of light is incident on a converging lens parallel to its principal axis. As one moves away from the lens on the other side of the principal axis, the intensity of light
explanation: The parallel beam, after refracting through lens, first converges to a point at the focus and then diverges. Hence the intensity of light first increases and then decreases.
subject: Physics

question: A candle placed $25\,cm$ from a lens  forms an image on a screen placed $75\,cm$ on the other side of the lens. The focal length and type of the lens should be
explanation:
According to Cartesian sign convention,
Object distance, $u = - 25\,{\rm{cm}}$
Image distance, $v = + 75\,{\rm{cm}}$
Using thin lens formula,
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{ + 75}} - \frac{1}{{ - 25}} = \frac{4}{{75}}$
$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{{75}}{4} = + 18.75\,{\rm{cm}}$
Since $f$ is positive, the lens is a convex lens.
subject: Physics

question: Radii of curvature of a converging lens are in the ratio 1: 2. Its focal length is $6\,cm$ and refractive index is 1.5. Then its radii of curvature are ________respectively.
explanation:
Let ${R_1} = R$
$\therefore {R_2} = 2R$
According to lens maker’s formula
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
here $f = 6\,cm,\mu = 1.5,$
For a convex lens,${R_1}$ and${R_2}$ have opposite signs in lensmaker’s formula.
$\therefore \frac{1}{6} = \left( {1.5 - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{{ - 2R}}} \right) = 0.5\left( {\frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{{2R}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6} = 0.5\left( {\frac{3}{{2R}}} \right) \Rightarrow R = 4.5\,{\rm{cm}}$
$\therefore {R_1} = 4.5\,{\rm{cm}},\,\,\,{R_2} = 2 \times 4.5 = 9\,{\rm{cm}}$
subject: Physics

question: The equi - convex lens has a focal length $'f'$. If the lens is cut along the line perpendicular to the principal axis and passing through the pole, what will be the focal length of any half part?
explanation:
The lens Maker’s formula is given as
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {{\mu _{med}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
Where, $f$ = focal length of lens, ${R_1}$ = radius of first curved part and
${R_2}$= radius of second curved part
As, for equiconvex lens ${R_1} = {R_2} = R\left( {say} \right)$
So, $\frac{1}{f} = \left( {{\mu _{real}} - 1} \right)\frac{2}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now, if lens is cut along the line perpendicular to the principal axis as shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955522/preptracker/questions/q_32070_explanation_1764955522035.png The new cut part of the lens ${R_1} = R$ and ${R_2} = \infty $
Again by using the lens maker’s formula, focal length of the new part of the lens
$\frac{1}{{f'}} = \left( {{\mu _{real}} - 1} \right)\left[ {\frac{1}{R} - \left( { - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{f'}} = \left( {{\mu _{real}} - 1} \right)\left[ {\frac{1}{R}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
So, from the eq.(1) and eq.(2) , we get
${\rm{f}}' = 2f$
subject: Physics

question: The following figure shows a beam of light converging at point $P$. When a concave lens of focal length $16\,cm$ is introduced in the path of the beam at a place shown by dotted line such that $OP$ becomes the axis of the lens, the beam converges at a distance $x$ from the lens.The value of $x$ will be equal to
explanation:
Here $OP$ can be considered as the object distance since the real image formed at $P$ will act as an object to the lens.
$\therefore \,\,u = OP = 12{\rm{cm}},\,\,f = - 16{\rm{cm}}{\rm{.}}$
To find image distance ($v$), using the thin lens formula,$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{ - 16}} + \frac{1}{{12}}$
$ \Rightarrow v = 48\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A convex and concave lens separated by 	distance $d$ are then put in contact. The focal length of the combination
explanation:
Focal length of a combination with separation (d) can be calculated as,$\frac{1}{{{F_1}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_2}}} - \frac{d}{{{f_1}{f_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Focal length of a combination without separation can be calculated as,
$\frac{1}{{{F_2}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2),
$\frac{1}{{{F_1}}} = \frac{1}{{{F_2}}} - \frac{d}{{{f_1}{f_2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{F_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{F_1}}} = \frac{d}{{{f_1}{f_2}}}$
Since ${f_1}$ and ${f_2}$ have opposite signs,
$\frac{d}{{{f_1}{f_2}}} < 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{F_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{F_1}}} < 0 \Rightarrow {F_2} > {F_1}$
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens made of glass  has focal length $0.15\,m$ in air. If the refractive index of glass is  3/2 and that of water is 4/3, the focal length of lens when immersed in water is
explanation:
Here, ${f_a} = 0.15m,{\mu _g} = 3/2,{\mu _w} = 4/3$
Using the lensmaker’s formula, when the lens is in air,
$\frac{1}{{{f_a}}} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _g}}}{{{\mu _a}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{(3{\rm{/}}2)}}{1} - 1} \right)C$
where $C = \left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{f_a}}} = \frac{C}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also, when the lens is in water,
$\frac{1}{{{f_w}}} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _g}}}{{{\mu _w}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{\left( {3/2} \right)}}{{\left( {4/3} \right)}} - 1} \right)C$
$\frac{1}{{{f_w}}} = \frac{C}{8}\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{{{f_w}}}{{{f_a}}} = \frac{C}{2} \times \frac{8}{C} = 4$
${f_w} = 4{f_a} = 4 \times 0.15 = 0.6\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Focal length of a convex lens is $20\,cm$ and its RI is 1.5. It produces an erect, enlarged image if the distance of the object from the lens is
explanation:
A convex lens produces an erect, enlarged image if the object lies between focus and
pole i.e., $\left| u \right| < f$ . Hence,$\left| u \right| = 15{\mkern 1mu} \,cm$ .
subject: Physics

question: The size of the image of  an object, which is at infinity, as formed by a convex lens of focal 	length $30\,cm$ is $2\,cm$. If a concave lens of focal length $20\,cm$ is  placed between the convex lens and the image at a distance of  $26\,cm$ from the lens, the new size of the image is
explanation:
The image formed by the convex lens at its focus acts as a virtual objcet for the concave lens.
Object distance for the concave lens
$u = 30 - 26 = 4\,{\mkern 1mu} cm$
Focal length of the concave lens $f = - 20\,cm$
Magnification $m = \frac{f}{{f + u}} = \frac{{ - 20}}{{ - 20 + 4}} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25$
Given, size of the image formed by the convex lens $ = 2\,cm$.
$\therefore $Size of the final image formed by the concave lens $ = 1.25 \times 2 = 2.5{\mkern 1mu} \,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens is made of 3 layers of glass of 3 different materials as in the figure. A point object is placed on its axis. The number of images of the object are
explanation: All three materials are distributed symmetrically about the optical axis of the lens. Hence each ray of light passing through the lens experiences similar refraction and  the combination acts as a single lens. Therefore, only one image of the point object is formed.
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens of focal length ‘$f$’ is placed in contact with a concave lens of the same focal length. The equivalent focal length of the combination is
explanation:
Given, focal length of convex lens,${f_1} = f$
and focal length of concave lens ${f_2} = - f$
The focal length of combination, when two lenses are in contact is given by
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Substituting given value in eq.(1), we get
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f} = 0 \Rightarrow f = \infty $
Hence, the equivalent focal length of the combination is infinity
subject: Physics

question: Two thin biconvex lens have focal lengths ${f_1}$ and ${f_2}$ . A third thin biconcave lens has focal length of ${f_3}$. If the first two biconvex lenses are in contact, the total power of the lenses is ${P_1}$ . If the first convex lens is in contact with the third lens, the total power is ${P_2}$ . If the second lens is in contact with the third lens, the total power is ${P_3}$ then
subject: Physics

question: For a given lens, the magnification was found to be twice as large as when the object was $0.15\,m$ distant from it as when the distance was $0.2\,m$ . The focal length of the lens is
explanation:
Given, ${u_1} = - 0.15{\mkern 1mu} \,m,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {u_2} = - 0.2\,m.$
Also, ${m_1} = 2{m_2} \Rightarrow \frac{f}{{f + {u_1}}} = 2\frac{f}{{f + {u_2}}}.$
$ \Rightarrow f = {u_2} - 2{u_1} = - 0.2 - 2 \times \left( { - 0.15} \right) = 0.1{\mkern 1mu} \,m$
subject: Physics

question: Two thin lenses have a combined powder of +9D. When they are separated by a distance of  $20\,cm$, their equivalent power becomes $ + \frac{{27}}{5}D$ . Their individual powers (in dioptres) are
explanation:
When two thin lenses of powers ${P_1}$ and ${P_2}$ are held in contact with each other, the power of the combination is given by
$P = {P_1} + {P_2}$
$\therefore 9 = {P_1} + {P_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When two thin lenses of power and are separated by a distance d, the equivalent power is given by
$P = {P_1} + {P_2} - d{P_1}{P_2}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{27}}{5} = {P_1} + {P_2} - \frac{{20}}{{100}}{P_1}{P_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{27}}{5} = 9 - \frac{1}{5}{P_1}{P_2}$ (using(1))
$ \Rightarrow {P_1}{P_2} = 18$ (2)
On solving (1) and (2), we get
${P_1} = 3D,\,\,{P_2} = 6D$
subject: Physics

question: A point source ${\rm{O}}$ kept at distance of $2R{\rm{ }}$ from pole ${\rm{ P}}$ of a concave interface having radius of curvature $R$ emits a beam at an angle of $30^\circ $ to the principle axis ${\rm{ PO}}$. The distance of the image of point source ${\rm{O}}$ from pole ${\rm{ P }}$is:
explanation:
As the distance $O$ & $P$ is $2R{\rm{ }}$ so the point $O$ is diametrically on the opposite side of the spherical surface. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949880/preptracker/questions/q_31676_explanation_1764949880093.png From Snell’s law
$2\sqrt 3 \sin 30^\circ = \sqrt 3 \sin C$
$ \Rightarrow r = 90^\circ $
From the right angle triangle $CPQ$
$\cos 60^\circ = \frac{R}{{CQ}} \Rightarrow CQ = 2R$
$ \Rightarrow PQ = R$
subject: Physics

question: One end of a cylindrical glass rod $\left( {\mu = 1.5} \right)$ is given the shape of a concave refracing surface of radius $10\,cm$ . An air bubble is situated in the glass rod at a point on its axis such that it appears to be at distance $10\,cm$ from the surface and inside glass when seen from the other medium. Find the actual location of air bubble, from the surface.
explanation:
Using ${\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{v} - \frac{{{\mu _1}}}{u} = \frac{{{\mu _2} - {\mu _1}}}{R}}$
Here, ${\mu _1} = 1.5,{\mu _2} = 1$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949877/preptracker/questions/q_31672_explanation_1764949877343.png ${v = - 10\,cm,R = + 10\,cm}$ (given)
$\therefore \quad \quad - \frac{1}{{10}} - \frac{{1.5}}{u} = \frac{{1 - 1.5}}{{10}} = - \frac{1}{{20}}$
${\frac{{1.5}}{u} = - \frac{1}{{10}} + \frac{1}{{20}} = - \frac{1}{{20}}\quad \therefore \quad u = - 30\,cm}$
Hence, the air bubble is actually located at a distance $30\,cm$ from the surface and inside glass.
subject: Physics

question:
A converging lens is used to form an image on a screen. When the upper half of the lens is covered by an opaque screen
1. Half of the image will disappear
2. No part of image will disappear
3. Intensity of the image will increase
4. Intensity of the image will decrease
explanation:
Some of the incident rays will be cut-off. So intensity of image will decrease. No part of the image will decrease.
So correct option is (4).

question: If a convex lens is dipped in a liquid whose refractive index is equal to the refractive index of the lens, then lens act like a
explanation:
In liquid medium
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _g}}}{{{\mu _l}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
As ${\mu _g} = {\mu _l},\,\,\frac{1}{f} = 0 \Rightarrow f = \infty $
For a glass plate $f = \infty $

question: Optic axis of a thin equiconvex lens is the${\rm{ x}}$-axis. The co-ordinates of a point object and its image are $( - 40cm,{\rm{ }}1cm)$ and $(50cm,{\rm{ }} - 2cm)$ respectively. Lens is located at :-
explanation:
The object and image are shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949872/preptracker/questions/q_31656_explanation_1764949872473.png From the figure $m = \frac{{h'}}{h} = - 2 = \frac{{\left| v \right|}}{{ - \left| u \right|}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left| v \right| = 2\left| u \right|$
${\left| v \right| + \left| u \right| = 90cm \Rightarrow \left| u \right| = 30\,cm}$
So the lens should be at $ - 10\,cm$

explanation: Only one converging point is found by this lens. Therefore, only one image is formed.

question:
Match the following
A. Lens of power${+ 1.0}\,D$
B. Lens of combination of power $ + 0.5\,D$ and $ + 0.5\,D$
C. Lens of power $ - \,2.5\,D$
D. Lenses combination of power $ - 4\,D$ and $ + 2\,D$
P. Convex lens of focal length $400\,cm$
Q . Convex lens of focal length $25\,cm$
R. Convex lens of focal length $100\,cm$
S. Concave lens of focal length $40\,cm$
explanation:
$\left( {A - R} \right)F = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{1}{1} = + 100\,cm$
$\left( {B - P} \right)\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{0.5}} + \frac{1}{{0.5}} = + 400\,cm$
${\left( {C - S} \right)F = - \frac{1}{{2.5}} = - 0.4 = - 40\,cm}$
$\left( {D - Q} \right)\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\,cm$
Option (2) is correct

options.text: A - R,B - P,C - S,	D - Q
A - S,  B - R,   C - P,D - Q
A - R, B - S,  C - Q,D - P

subject: Physics

question: A convex lens of focal length $12\,cm$ is made of glass of $\mu  = \frac{3}{2}.$  What will be its focal length when immersed in liquid of $\mu  = \frac{5}{4}?$
explanation:
$\frac{{{f_2}}}{{{f_1}}} = \frac{{\left( {{\mu _g} - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\mu _g}}}{{{\mu _l}}} - 1} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{3}{2} - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{3/2}}{{5/4}} - 1} \right)}} = \frac{5}{2}$
$\therefore {f_2} = {f_1}\left( {\frac{5}{2}} \right) = \frac{{12 \times 5}}{2} = 30cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Lens which is Placed in a Transparent Medium

explanation: Air bubble has air whose refractive index is less than refractive index of water so it will behave as concave lens.

explanation:

question: A narrow parallel beam of light is incident paraxially on a solid transparent sphere of  radius $r$  kept in air. What should be the refractive index if the beam is to be focused at the farther surface of the sphere.
explanation:
$\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{v} - \frac{{{\mu _1}}}{u} = \frac{{{\mu _2} - {\mu _1}}}{R}$
For refraction at first surface
${\mu _1} = 1\quad {\mu _2} = \mu $
$u = \infty \quad R = R$
$v = 2R$
$\frac{\mu }{{2R}} - \frac{1}{\infty } = \frac{{\mu - 1}}{R}$
$\frac{\mu }{2} = \mu - 1 \Rightarrow \mu = 1.5$
subject: Physics

question: A point object is placed at the centre of a glass of radius  $6\,cm$  and refractive index and refractive index 1.5. The distance of the virtual image from the surface of the sphere is
explanation:
Using refraction formula
$\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{v} - \frac{{{\mu _1}}}{u} = \frac{{{\mu _2} - {\mu _1}}}{R}$
$\frac{{1.5}}{v} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{{1.5 - 1}}{{ - 6}}$
in given case, medium (1) is glass and (2) is air
${ \Rightarrow v = - 6\,cm}$
subject: Physics

question: A point object $O$ is placed in front of glass rod having spherical end of radius of curvature $30\,cm$. The image would be formed at
explanation:
By using formula $\frac{{{\mu _2}}}{v} - \frac{{{\mu _1}}}{u} = \frac{{{\mu _2} - {\mu _1}}}{R}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{1.5}}{v} - \frac{1}{{( - 15)}} = \frac{{(1.5 - 1)}}{{ + 30}} \Rightarrow v = - 30\,cm.$
Negative sign shows that, image is obtained on the same side of object i.e., towards left.
subject: Physics

question: A convex lens has a focal length $f$. It is cut into two parts along the dotted line as shown in the figure. The focal length of each part will be
explanation:
Before cutting the lens
$\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{R}} \right)} \right)$
$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{{2(\mu - 1)}}{R}$
After cutting the lens $\frac{1}{{f'}} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right)$
$\frac{1}{{f'}} = \frac{{\left( {\mu - 1} \right)}}{R} \Rightarrow f' = 2f$
subject: Physics

explanation:

In water medium
${P_2} = \frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _g}}}{{{\mu _w}}} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
In water medium power of the lens decreases

question: When a biconvex lens of glass having refractive index 1.47 is dipped in a liquid, it acts as a plane sheet of glass. This implies that the liquid must have refractive index
explanation:

Here$f = \infty $ so $\frac{1}{f} = 0$ $\therefore $ ${\mu _g} = {\mu _m}$

question: An object is kept at a distance of  $16\,cm$ from a thin lens and the image formed is real. If the object is kept at a distance of  $6\,cm$ from the same lens, the image formed is virtual. If the sizes of the image formed are equal the focal length of the lens will be
explanation:
As, a convex lens alone can form a real image as well as a virtual image, therefore, the lens is a convex lens. Let, $f$ be the focal length of the lens and $m$ be the magnification produced.
In the first case, when image is real,
${u = - 16{\mkern 1mu} cm,}$${v{\rm{ = }}\left( {m \times 16} \right)cm}$
As $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{{16m}} + \frac{1}{{16}} = \frac{1}{f}$${ \Rightarrow 1 + \frac{1}{m} = \frac{{16}}{f}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)}$
In the second case, when image is virtual.
${u = - 6{\mkern 1mu} \,c{\mkern 1mu} m,}$${v\, = \left( { - 6\,m} \right)cm}$
From $\frac{1}{{v{\rm{ }}}} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{{ - 6{\rm{m}}}} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{f}$${ \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{1}{m} = \frac{6}{f}\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)}$
Add. Eq (1) and Eq. (2), we have
$2 = \frac{{22}}{f}$ ${ \Rightarrow f = \frac{{22}}{2} = 11\,cm}$
subject: Physics

question: One surface of a lens is convex and the other is concave. If the radius of curvatures are ${r_1}$  and ${r_2}$  respectively, the lens will be convex, if
explanation:
As, $\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{r_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_2}}}} \right)$ (Lens maker formula)
For lens to be convex, $\left( {\frac{1}{{{r_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_2}}}} \right) > 0$
$\frac{1}{{{r_1}}} > \frac{1}{{{r_2}}}$$ \Rightarrow {r_1} < {r_2}$
subject: Physics

question: A concave lens of focal length  $20\,cm$ produces an image half in size of the real object. The distance of  the real object is
explanation:
As, $m = \frac{f}{{f + u}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\frac{1}{2} = \frac{{ - 20}}{{ - 20 + u}}$
$ - 20 + u = - 40$$ \Rightarrow u = - 20\,{\rm{cm}}$

question: A thin lens has focal length,  $f$  and its aperture has diametert forms an image of intensity  $I$. Now the central part of the aperture upto diameter  $\frac{d}{2}$  is blocked by an opaque paper. The focal length and image intensity will change to
explanation:
Focal length will remain same. Intensity is directly proportional to the area of the aperture of the lens receiving the light.
Total area of aperture is $A = \frac{{\pi {d^2}}}{4}$
Remaining area is
$A' = \frac{{\pi {d^2}}}{4} - \frac{{\pi {d^2}}}{{16}} = \frac{{3\pi {d^2}}}{{16}}$
$\frac{I}{{I'}} = \frac{A}{{A'}} = \frac{4}{3},I' = \frac{3}{4}I$
subject: Physics

question: Two convex lenses of focal lengths ${f_1}$ and  ${f_2}$ are mounted coaxially separated by a distance. If the power of the combination is zero, the distance between the lenses is
subject: Physics

question: A plastic hemisphere has a radius of curvature of  $8\,cm$ and an index of refraction of 1.6. On the axis halfway between the plane surface and the spherical one ( $4\,cm$ from each) is a small object ${\rm{O}}$. The distance between the two images when viewed along the axis from the two sides of the hemisphere is approximately
explanation:
Distance of image from the plane surface is:
${x_1} = \frac{4}{{1.6}} = 2.5\,cm$$\left( {{d_{app}} = \frac{{{d_{actual}}}}{\mu }} \right)$
For the curved side$\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}$
${n_1} = 1.6,\,{n_2} = 1$
$R = {\rm{ }}8{\rm{ }}cm$
$\frac{{1.6}}{4} + \frac{1}{{{x_2}}} = \frac{{1 - 1.6}}{8}$
$\therefore \,{x_2} \approx - 3.0\,cm$
The minus sign means the image is virtual
$\therefore \,{I_1}{I_2} = \left( {8 - 2.5 - 3.0} \right)cm = 2.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A ray of light falls on a transparent sphere with centre at ${\rm{C }}$ as shown in figure. The ray emerges from the sphere parallel to line $AB$. The refractive index of the sphere is
explanation:
The path traced by the light ray is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949828/preptracker/questions/q_31119_explanation_1764949828102.png By applying Snell’s law at $A$ and $B$
$\sin 60^\circ = \mu \sin r$
$\mu \sin r = \sin 2r$
From the above equation’s we get
$r = 30^\circ \& \mu = \sqrt 3 $
subject: Physics

question: A lens is placed between a source of light and a wall. It forms image of area ${{\rm{A}}_1}$ and ${{\rm{A}}_2}$ on the wall for its two different positions. The area of the source or light is
explanation:
${m_1} = \frac{{{A_1}}}{A}$ and ${m_2} = \frac{{{A_2}}}{A}$
Where $A$ is the area of the object
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}{m_2} = \frac{{{A_1}{A_2}}}{A}$
Also it can be proved that ${m_1}{m_2} = 1$
$ \Rightarrow A = \sqrt {{A_1}{A_2}} $
subject: Physics

question: If two lenses of  +5 dioptres are mounted at some distance apart, the equivalent power will always be negative if the distance is
explanation:
Using $P = {P_1} + {P_2} - d \times {P_1}{P_2}$
For equivalent power to be negative
$d \times {P_1}{P_2} > {P_1} + {P_2} \Rightarrow d \times 25 > 10$
$ \Rightarrow d > \frac{{10}}{{25}}m \Rightarrow d > \frac{{10 \times 100}}{{25}} \Rightarrow d > 40\,cm.$
subject: Physics

question: A thin lens of material having refractive index $\mu  = 1.5$ and focal length of  $20\,cm$ when placed in air is taken and two mediums of different refractive indices ${\mu _1} = 1.2$ and ${\mu _2} = 2.5$ are covering upper and lower halves of the lens, respectively as shown in figure. If an object is placed on the principal axis, then its two images will be formed. Consider the object to be at $\infty $, the separation between two images formed would be:
explanation:
Focal length for upper half is
${f_1} = \left( {\frac{{\mu - 1}}{{\mu /{\mu _1} - 1}}} \right){f_{air}} = \left( {\frac{{1.5 - 1}}{{\frac{{1.5}}{{1.2}} - 1}}} \right)20 = 40\,cm$
Focal length for lower half is,
${f_2} = \left( {\frac{{\mu - 1}}{{\mu /{\mu _2} - 1}}} \right){f_{air}} = \left( {\frac{{1.5 - 1}}{{\frac{{1.5}}{{2.5}} - 1}}} \right) \times 20 = - 25\,cm$
If the object is at infinity, two images will form at corresponding focuses.
So, the required separation is
$x = \left| {{f_1}} \right| + \left| {{f_2}} \right| = 40 + 25 = 65\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Refraction through a Lens which is Placed in a Transparent Medium

question: A real image is formed by a convex lens, when it is cemented with a concave lens, again the real image is formed. The real image
explanation:
Let ${f_1}\& {f_2}$are the focal lengths of convex and concave lens. When these two lens are combined then
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = \frac{{{f_2} - {f_1}}}{{{f_1}{f_2}}}$
Here ${f_2} > {f_1}$ since $F$ is given as (+) ve
$F = \frac{{{f_1}{f_2}}}{{{f_2} - {f_1}}} > {f_1}$
i.e., the image shifts away from the lens

options.text: Shifts away from the lens system

subject: Physics

question: Figure given below shows a beam of light converging at point ${\rm{P}}$.  When a concave lens of focal length  ${\rm{16}}\,{\rm{cm}}$ is introduced in the path of the beam at a place $O$ shown by dotted line such that $OP$  becomes the axis of the lens, the beam converges at a distance $x$ from the lens. The value $x$ will be equal to
explanation:
By using lens formula
$\frac{1}{{ - 16}} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{{\left( {12} \right)}}$
$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{8} - \frac{1}{{16}} = \frac{{2 - 1}}{{16}} \Rightarrow v = 16\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Two similar plano convex lenses are combined together in three different ways as shown in the adjoining figure. The ratio of the focal lengths in three cases will be
explanation:
Focal length of a plano convex lens of radius of curvature R and refractive index $\mu $ is
For a plano convex lens. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949822/preptracker/questions/q_31091_explanation_1764949822507.png $\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{1}{{ - R}}} \right) = \frac{{\mu - 1}}{R}$
Even for the convexo plane lens https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949823/preptracker/questions/q_31091_explanation_1764949823114.png $\frac{1}{f} = \mu - 1\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right) = \frac{{\mu - 1}}{R}$
For all the three given combinations
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{f} = \frac{{2(\mu - 1)}}{R}$

$ \Rightarrow {F_1}:{F_2}:{F_3} = 1:1:1$
subject: Physics

question: A thin plano convex lens $\left( {\mu = 5/4} \right)$ and if plane surface of plano convex lens is silvered, then a plano concave lens $\mu = 3/2$ are combined together. The radius of curvature of the curved surface is $30\,cm$.
explanation:
The effective focal length is
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{{{f_m}}} - \frac{2}{{{f_l}}}$
Let ${L_1}\& {L_2}$are two lens. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949821/preptracker/questions/q_31080_explanation_1764949821069.png ${f_m} = \infty $(For plane surface)
For${L_1}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_1}}} = \left( {\frac{3}{2} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{\infty } - \frac{1}{{30}}} \right) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{60}}$
For ${L_2}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = \left( {\frac{5}{4} - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{30}} - \frac{1}{\infty }} \right) = \frac{1}{{120}}$
$\frac{1}{{{f_l}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{f_2}}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{60}} + \frac{1}{{120}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{120}}$
$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{\infty } + \frac{2}{{120}} = \frac{1}{{160}}$
$F = 60\,cm$
So the system behaves like a convex mirror

options.text: System behaves like a convex mirror
System behaves like a concave mirror
Focal length of the system is 20 cm

question: Consider a glass slab which is silvered at one side and the other side is transparent. Given the refractive index of the glass slab to be 1.5. explanation:

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958182/preptracker/questions/q_112891_explanation_1764958182038.png
Therefore the final refraction angle will be $45^{\circ}$ and the total deviation is $\left(45^{\circ}+45^{\circ}\right)=90^{\circ}$.

question: Match the terms and conditions related to total internal reflection (Column I) with their corresponding descriptions (Column II). Choose the correct option from the given codes.
explanation:
(A) Total Internal Reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle, which means that all the light is reflected back into the denser medium without any refraction into the rarer medium.
(B) The Critical Angle $\left(i_{c}\right)$ is defined as the angle of incidence for which the angle of refraction is $90^{\circ}$, beyond which total internal reflection occurs.
(C) Snell's Law describes the relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction for a given pair of media and the refractive indices of two media.
(D) The Refractive Index ($n_{21}$) is less than one when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium, as per the condition for total internal reflection to occur. Correct option is (3)

question: Match the terms and concepts related to refraction (Column I) with their corresponding definitions or descriptions (Column II). Choose the correct option from the given codes.
explanation:
(A) Snell's Law is the relationship that defines how the direction of a light beam changes when passing from one medium to another, relating the angle of incidence and refraction with the refractive indices of the two media
(B) The Refractive Index $\left(n_{21}\right)$ is a constant characteristic of a pair of media which is independent of the angle of incidence .
(C) An Optically Denser Medium is a medium in which the refracted ray bends towards the normal, indicating that this medium has a higher refractive index than the medium from which the light is coming .
(D) The Angle of Refraction ($r$) is less than the angle of incidence ($i$) if the refractive index of the second medium ($n_{21}$) is greater than 1 , showing that the light is bending towards the normal as it passes into a denser medium. Correct option is (2)
